- Structured, English-like syntax with variables, loops, conditionals.
- Examples:
  - **VAL-II** (Unimation) — Variable Assembly Language.
  - **AML** (IBM) — A Manufacturing Language.
  - **RAPT** (University of Edinburgh) — Rule-based, with geometric reasoning.
  - **AL** (Stanford) — Actions, Assertions, Expressions.
  - **KAREL** (FANUC) — Pascal-like structured language.
  - **RAPID** (ABB) — Modern structured language.
  - **KRL** (KUKA Robot Language).
  - **PDL2** (Comau).

#### Generation 4 — Offline / Simulation-Based
- Programs written and tested in a virtual 3D environment.
- Examples: **ROBOGUIDE (FANUC)**, **RobotStudio (ABB)**, **KUKA.Sim**, **Delmia**, **Visual Components**.

#### Generation 5 — AI / Task-Level / Autonomous
- The programmer specifies **goals**; the planner generates paths.
- Uses techniques from AI, ML, and classical planning.
- Examples: ROS (Robot Operating System) with MoveIt!, Python with PyRobot, behavior trees.

### 2.2 The Two Programming Paradigms in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 2 frequently asks this as a 7-mark question.**

| Paradigm | Description | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|:---------|:------------|:----------|:-----------|
| **Online Programming** | Program created *on* the actual robot using teach pendant or live terminal | Real-world accuracy, immediate feedback | Robot downtime, unsafe for complex tasks, no offline optimization |
| **Offline Programming (OLP)** | Program created in a 3D simulation environment, then downloaded to robot | Zero downtime, complex paths possible, safe testing, parallel engineering | Calibration drift between simulation and reality, requires accurate CAD models |

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Modern Robot Program

A contemporary high-level robot program contains **six logical blocks**. KTU examiners call this the **"Six-Block Program Structure"** (adapted from Corke’s *Robotics, Vision and Control*):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block 1 — Initialization / Setup**
> Define tool frame, base frame, work object frame, speed overrides, payload, coordinate system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block 2 — Sensor / I/O Configuration**
> Declare digital inputs, digital outputs, analog signals, and interrupts.

> **[Block 3 — Motion Planning]**
> Specify approach points, target poses, trajectory types (linear, joint, circular, spline).

> **[Block 4 — Logic / Branching]**
> IF-THEN-ELSE for sensor-driven decisions; WHILE / FOR loops for repetitive tasks.

> **[Block 5 — Gripper / Tool Actions]**
> Open/close gripper, energize welding torch, activate dispenser, etc.

> **[Block 6 — Termination / Safe-State]**
> Return to home position, deactivate tools, signal task completion to PLC.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every numerical, conceptual, and structural fact a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. **No `|` symbol is used inside cells** — we use `\vert` and `\mid` to remain LaTeX-clean.

| Concept | Standard / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|:--------|:-------------------|:---------------|:---------------------|
| **Pose in Cartesian Space** | $P = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ | Position $(x,y,z)$ + Roll $\phi$ + Pitch $\theta$ + Yaw $\psi$ | Meters + Radians |
| **Joint-Space Vector** | $q = (q_{1}, q_{2}, \dots, q_{n}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Generalized joint coordinates | Degrees or Radians |
| **Forward Kinematics** | $T_{end} = f(q)$ | End-effector transform from joint vector | Homogeneous $4\times4$ matrix |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | $q = f^{-1}(T_{end})$ | Joint vector from desired transform | May have multiple solutions |
| **Linear Motion Speed** | $v = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ | Tool Centre Point (TCP) linear speed | $\vert v \vert \le 2 \text{ m/s}$ typical industrial |
| **Joint Velocity Limit** | $\dot{q}_{i} \le \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ | Per-joint angular speed cap | $90^{\circ}\text{/s}$ to $400^{\circ}\text{/s}$ industrial |
| **Path Accuracy (ISO 9283)** | $AP = \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \bar{x}_{i} - x_{i,\text{cmd}} \vert^{2} }$ | Mean squared deviation from commanded path | mm |
| **Pose Repeatability** | $RP = \bar{x} \pm 3\sigma_{pose}$ | Mean pose $\pm$ 3 standard deviations | $\pm 0.02$ mm high-precision |
| **Program Cycle Time** | $T_{c} = T_{motion} + T_{IO} + T_{wait}$ | Total task time | Seconds |
| **Payload Ratio** | $\mu = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{rated}}$ | Actual $\div$ rated load | $\mu \le 1.0$ (never exceed) |
| **Singularity Condition** | $\det(J(q)) = 0$ | Jacobian loses rank at singular configs | Dimensional scalar |
| **D-H Parameter Set** | $(a_{i}, \alpha_{i}, d_{i}, \theta_{i})$ | Link length, twist, offset, angle | Link $i$ of $n$ |

### 2.5 Basic Programming Constructs (the "Six Siblings")

Every robot language, from VAL-II to RAPID to KRL, supports the following six fundamental constructs. KTU Module 2 lists these explicitly:

1. **Variable Declaration and Assignment**
   - $v \leftarrow v + \Delta v$ style increment operators.
2. **Sequential Execution** — instructions executed in listed order.
3. **Selection (Conditional Branching)** — IF / THEN / ELSE / CASE structures.
4. **Iteration (Loops)** — FOR, WHILE, REPEAT-UNTIL.
5. **Subroutines / Procedures** — modular reusable code blocks.
6. **Interrupts / Event Handlers** — asynchronous responses to sensor signals.

### 2.6 Five Common Programming Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **These five techniques are the heart of Module 2.** KTU expects you to be able to compare and contrast them in a 14-mark question.

#### Technique 1 — Teach-Pendant Programming (Lead-Through)
The operator manually jogs the robot through desired positions using a handheld controller. Positions are recorded as named *waypoints*. The robot replays the sequence.

- **Pros**: Intuitive, no syntax knowledge required, accurate for simple tasks.
- **Cons**: Robot is occupied; complex trajectories are tedious; no conditional logic possible.

#### Technique 2 — Text-Based High-Level Programming
Programs are written in languages like RAPID, KRL, KAREL, or VAL-II. Full algorithmic control is available.

- **Pros**: Complex logic, sensor integration, reusable libraries, version control.
- **Cons**: Requires skilled programmer; syntax errors cause downtime.

#### Technique 3 — Offline Programming (OLP) with Simulation
Programs developed entirely in a virtual 3D cell using software like ABB RobotStudio or FANUC ROBOGUIDE.

- **Pros**: Zero production downtime, optimization possible, complex paths achievable, safe.
- **Cons**: Calibration mismatch; need accurate CAD models; higher initial cost.

#### Technique 4 — Graphical / Icon-Based Programming
Drag-and-drop block-based programming. Example: **LEGO Mindstorms EV3-G**, **Universal Robots URScript Polyscope**, **DobotBlock**.

- **Pros**: Low entry barrier, visual clarity, good for education and small businesses.
- **Cons**: Limited scalability; not suited to complex industrial tasks.

#### Technique 5 — Task-Level / AI-Based Programming
The user declares high-level goals; the system plans motions automatically. Examples: **ROS MoveIt!**, **Behavior Trees**, **STRIPS planners**, **LLM-driven task planners (2024 trend)**.

- **Pros**: Highest abstraction; handles unstructured environments.
- **Cons**: Computationally expensive; not yet deterministic enough for many industrial uses.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding lines** → 95% of programs written in OLP (KUKA.Sim, ABB RobotStudio).
- **Pharmaceutical pick-and-place** → VAL-II / RAPID on SCARA robots.
- **Academic research** → ROS + Python + MoveIt!.
- **Education (KTU lab)** → LEGO EV3, Arduino, or CoppeliaSim with Python.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Comparison Matrix & Analytical Walk-Through

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics Representation of a Programmed Motion

When a programmer specifies a Cartesian target $T_{target}$, the robot controller must compute joint angles $q$ such that:

$$T_{target} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}(q_{i})$$

**Step 1** — *Define the homogeneous transform of a single link* using Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameters:

$$A_{i} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_{i} & -\sin\theta_{i}\cos\alpha_{i} & \sin\theta_{i}\sin\alpha_{i} & a_{i}\cos\theta_{i} \\
\sin\theta_{i} & \cos\theta_{i}\cos\alpha_{i} & -\cos\theta_{i}\sin\alpha_{i} & a_{i}\sin\theta_{i} \\
0 & \sin\alpha_{i} & \cos\alpha_{i} & d_{i} \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2** — *Combine the link transforms* for an $n$-joint robot (typically $n = 6$ for industrial arms):

$$T_{0}^{n}(q) = A_{1}(q_{1}) \cdot A_{2}(q_{2}) \cdot A_{3}(q_{3}) \cdot A_{4}(q_{4}) \cdot A_{5}(q_{5}) \cdot A_{6}(q_{6})$$

**Step 3** — *Extract the end-effector pose*:

$$T_{end} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{3\times3} & p_{3\times1} \\
0_{1\times3} & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

where $R$ is the orientation rotation matrix and $p = (x, y, z)^{T}$ is the position vector.

**Step 4** — *Verify the pose matches the programmed target*:

$$\varepsilon = \Vert T_{target} - T_{computed} \Vert_{F} \le \varepsilon_{tol}$$

If the Frobenius norm $\varepsilon$ exceeds the tolerance, the controller flags a *singularity* or *unreachable-point* error.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through: From "Move to P1" to Servo Command

Suppose a programmer writes the high-level instruction `MOVE P1` in RAPID-style syntax.

**Step A** — *The interpreter parses the waypoint name* `P1` and looks up the stored Cartesian pose $T_{P1} = (x,y,z, q_{x}, q_{y}, q_{z}, q_{w})$.

**Step B** — *The motion planner invokes the inverse kinematics solver*:

$$q_{desired} = f^{-1}(T_{P1})$$

**Step C** — *The trajectory generator interpolates* between the current pose $T_{current}$ and $T_{P1}$ over a time interval $\Delta t$, producing a sequence of intermediate waypoints $T(t_{k})$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N$.

**Step D** — *A velocity profile* (trapezoidal or S-curve) is applied:

$$
v(t) =
\begin{cases}
a \cdot t & 0 \le t \le t_{acc} \\
v_{max} & t_{acc} \le t \le t_{dec} \\
v_{max} - a \cdot (t - t_{dec}) & t_{dec} \le t \le t_{total}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step E** — *The joint controller issues servo commands* every $\Delta t_{servo} \approx 1$ ms:

$$\tau_{i}(t) = K_{p} \cdot e_{i}(t) + K_{d} \cdot \dot{e}_{i}(t) + K_{i} \cdot \int e_{i}(t)\, dt$$

where $e_{i}(t) = q_{i,desired}(t) - q_{i,actual}(t)$ is the position error of joint $i$.

This five-step chain is the **conceptual core** of every robot program, regardless of language.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Programming Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix verbatim in 14-mark answers** for full marks. It satisfies the KTU "compare and contrast" question style.

| Criterion | Teach-Pendant | Text-Based High-Level | Offline (OLP) | Graphical/Icon | Task-Level / AI |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------------|:--------------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Abstraction Level** | Low (joint-centric) | Medium (Cartesian or joint) | Medium-High | Medium | Very High |
| **Required Skill** | Operator training | Programming literacy | CAD + programming | Basic computer literacy | AI / robotics expert |
| **Robot Downtime** | High (robot used to program) | Moderate (compile-test cycles) | Near-zero | Low | Moderate |
| **Program Complexity** | Simple | High | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Sensor Integration** | None / minimal | Full (I/O, vision, force) | Full | Limited | Full |
| **Cost (Initial Setup)** | Low | Low-Moderate | High (CAD + software licence) | Low | Very High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Used in KTU Labs?** | Yes (FANUC pendant) | Yes (RAPID, KRL) | Sometimes (CoppeliaSim) | Yes (LEGO EV3) | Emerging (ROS2) |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Often probabilistic |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Writing a Simple Pick-and-Place Program

Let us build a pseudo-code program using generic robot-language syntax (representative of RAPID or KAREL).

**Step 1** — Declare variables and constants

```
CONST robtarget pPick := [[200, 100, 50], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pPlace := [[400, 200, 50], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pHome := [[0, 0, 400], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
VAR num cycleCount := 0;
```

**Step 2** — Define a subroutine for picking

```
PROC PickPart()
    MoveJ pApproach, v500, fine, gripper;
    MoveL pPick, v100, fine, gripper;
    SetDO gripperClose, 1;
    WaitTime 0.5;
    MoveL pApproach, v500, fine, gripper;
ENDPROC
```

**Step 3** — Define the main loop

```
PROC main()
    WHILE cycleCount < 100 DO
        PickPart();
        MoveJ pPlaceApproach, v500, fine, gripper;
        MoveL pPlace, v100, fine, gripper;
        SetDO gripperClose, 0;
        WaitTime 0.5;
        MoveL pPlaceApproach, v500, fine, gripper;
        MoveJ pHome, v1000, fine, gripper;
        cycleCount := cycleCount + 1;
    ENDWHILE
ENDPROC
```

**Step 4** — Compute the cycle time

$$T_{cycle} = T_{approach} + T_{pick} + T_{transfer} + T_{place} + T_{return}$$

For typical industrial speeds: $T_{cycle} \approx 6$ seconds, yielding $\dfrac{3600}{6} = 600$ parts/hour.

### 3.5 Derivation of the Jacobian Singularity Condition (for Programmer Awareness)

When the Jacobian matrix $J(q)$ loses rank, the robot is in a **singular configuration**, and the programmer must avoid such waypoints.

**Step 1** — *Jacobian definition* (linear velocity of end-effector):

$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$$

**Step 2** — *Singularity condition*:

$$\det(J(q)) = 0$$

**Step 3** — *Consequence*: at a singularity, infinite joint velocities would be required to produce a finite Cartesian velocity — physically impossible.

**Step 4** — *Programmer's defense*: choose target poses that lie inside the **dexterous workspace**, defined as:

$$W_{dex} = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^{3} \mid \text{rank}(J(q)) = n \text{ for all } q = f^{-1}(x) \}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Robot Programming Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L5[Level 5 - Task Level AI] --> L4[Level 4 - Offline OLP]
    L4 --> L3[Level 3 - High Level Textual]
    L3 --> L2[Level 2 - Teach Pendant Lead Through]
    L2 --> L1[Level 1 - Joint Servo Level]
    L1 --> HW[Robot Hardware]

    L5a[ROS MoveIt - Python] --> L5
    L4a[ABB RobotStudio - FANUC ROBOGUIDE] --> L4
    L3a[RAPID - KRL - VAL II - KAREL] --> L3
    L2a[FANUC iPendant - ABB FlexPendant] --> L2
    L1a[Servo C Code - DSP Firmware] --> L1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Modern Robot Programming Environment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEV[Development Environment]
        A1[Graphical IDE] --> A2[Text Editor]
        A2 --> A3[Compiler or Interpreter]
        A3 --> A4[3D Simulation Engine]
    end

    subgraph MID[Middleware Layer]
        B1[Task Planner] --> B2[Motion Planner IK Solver]
        B2 --> B3[Trajectory Generator]
        B3 --> B4[Controller Interface]
    end

    subgraph HW[Robot Hardware]
        C1[Motor Drivers] --> C2[Encoders and Sensors]
        C2 --> C3[End Effector Actuators]
    end

    A4 --> B1
    B4 --> C1
    C2 --> B4
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing a Programming Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Task Complexity} -->|Simple Pick and Place| R1[Teach Pendant]
    Q1 -->|Moderate With Sensors| R2[Text Based RAPID KRL]
    Q1 -->|High Mix Low Volume| R3[Offline OLP]
    Q1 -->|Unstructured AI Driven| R4[Task Level ROS]

    R1 --> E1[Low Cost Fast Setup]
    R2 --> E2[High Control Good Logic]
    R3 --> E3[Zero Downtime CAD Linked]
    R4 --> E4[Autonomous Adaptive]
```

### 4.4 Program Execution Pipeline (Six-Block Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Block 1 Initialization Frame Setup] --> P2[Block 2 Sensor IO Config]
    P2 --> P3[Block 3 Motion Planning]
    P3 --> P4[Block 4 Logic Branching]
    P4 --> P5[Block 5 Tool Action Gripper]
    P5 --> P6[Block 6 Termination Safe State]
    P6 --> P1
```

### 4.5 Logical Architecture of Robot Language Generations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Gen 1 Assembly - VAL] --> G2[Gen 2 Teach Pendant]
    G2 --> G3[Gen 3 High Level AML AL RAPT]
    G3 --> G4[Gen 4 Offline OLP]
    G4 --> G5[Gen 5 AI Task Level LLM Planners]
    G5 -.Future.-> G6[Gen 6 Cognitive Autonomous]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert** — In 14-mark questions on robot programming, students commonly lose marks for:
> 1. **Confusing "programming language" with "programming technique."** A *language* is the syntax; a *technique* is the methodology (teach-pendant, OLP, etc.).
> 2. **Forgetting to name at least three specific languages** (e.g., RAPID, KRL, AML) when asked to discuss high-level languages.
> 3. **Omitting the six-block program structure** — the KTU marking scheme allocates 2 marks for this exact sequence.
> 4. **Using `Online Programming` and `Teach-Pendant Programming` interchangeably.** They are related but not identical.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define robot programming. List the three levels at which a robot can be programmed.**

**Model Answer:**

Robot programming is the process of creating a structured set of instructions that direct a robot's motion, sensor interaction, and tool action to perform a specified task.

The three levels are:
- **Joint-level programming** — direct specification of individual joint angles and velocities.
- **Manipulator-level / end-effector programming** — specification in Cartesian space; the controller computes joint values using inverse kinematics.
- **Task-level / object-level programming** — high-level description of *what* is to be accomplished, with the system planning the *how*.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three levels with brief explanation.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between online and offline robot programming. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Online Programming | Offline Programming (OLP) |
|:-------|:-------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Where developed** | On the actual robot | On a PC in a 3D simulation environment |
| **Robot downtime** | Yes | No |
| **Tools used** | Teach pendant, handheld terminal | ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, KUKA.Sim |
| **Example** | Programming a FANUC LR-Mate using its iPendant | Programming a KUKA KR-16 in KUKA.Sim with full cell simulation |

*(3 marks: clear comparison + 1 example each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**. Provide complete, step-by-step model solutions for every sub-part. KTU requires both *conceptual clarity* and *technical depth* for full marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **five generations of robot programming languages** with one example language for each generation.

**Q: (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **six-block structure of a modern robot program**. State one KTU-recommended practice for each block.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Generations of Robot Programming Languages (7 marks)**

| Generation | Characteristics | Example |
|:-----------|:----------------|:--------|
| 1 — Assembly / Hardware Level | Direct register / joint-angle commands; no abstraction | Original **VAL** (Unimation, 1979) |
| 2 — Teach Pendant / Lead-Through | Operator-guided motion capture; replay-only logic | **FANUC iPendant**, **ABB FlexPendant** |
| 3 — High-Level Textual | Structured syntax with variables, loops, conditionals | **RAPID (ABB)**, **KRL (KUKA)**, **AML (IBM)**, **KAREL (FANUC)** |
| 4 — Offline / Simulation-Based | Programs built in 3D virtual cells; downloaded to controller | **RobotStudio**, **ROBOGUIDE**, **KUKA.Sim** |
| 5 — Task-Level / AI-Based | Goal specification; autonomous planning | **ROS MoveIt!**, **Behavior Trees**, **LLM-driven planners (2024)** |

*(7 marks: 1 per generation + 1 mark for chronological coherence.)*

**(b) Six-Block Program Structure (7 marks)**

```
Block 1: Initialization / Setup
   - Practice: Always define tool frame, base frame, workobject frame,
     payload, and coordinate system explicitly.

Block 2: Sensor / I/O Configuration
   - Practice: Configure digital I/O with debounce; declare interrupts
     with priorities.

Block 3: Motion Planning
   - Practice: Use approach points offset 50 mm above the target;
     choose motion type (linear vs joint) by geometry.

Block 4: Logic / Branching
   - Practice: Wrap sensor reads in IF-ELSE with safe fallback poses.

Block 5: Gripper / Tool Action
   - Practice: Use a feedback signal (e.g., gripper-closed sensor)
     before continuing motion.

Block 6: Termination / Safe-State
   - Practice: Always return to a defined home position; signal
     PLC for cycle-end before exiting the program.
```

*(7 marks: 1 mark per block for the description, 1 mark for the neat diagram / sequence.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the **five major robot programming techniques** in a matrix. Recommend the best technique for (i) a high-volume automotive welding line and (ii) a research lab prototyping a humanoid assistant.

**Q: (b)** Write a **generic pick-and-place robot program** in pseudo-code using the six basic programming constructs. Estimate the cycle time if the linear travel between pick and place is **400 mm** and the maximum TCP speed is **1 m/s**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Matrix + Recommendations (7 marks)**

| Technique | Strength | Weakness | Best For |
|:----------|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| Teach-Pendant | Simple, accurate | Downtime, no logic | Short-run teaching |
| Text-Based High-Level | Full control, sensors | Needs programmer | Standard industrial cells |
| Offline (OLP) | Zero downtime, complex paths | Cost, calibration | High-volume production |
| Graphical / Icon | Easy to learn | Limited scalability | Education, SMEs |
| Task-Level / AI | Most abstract, adaptive | Non-deterministic, slow | Research, dynamic environments |

**Recommendation for (i) high-volume automotive welding line** → **Offline Programming (OLP)**, because it eliminates downtime, supports complex multi-robot cell simulation, and is industry standard for welding.

**Recommendation for (ii) humanoid research lab** → **Task-Level / AI-Based Programming** with ROS and behavior trees, because the environment is unstructured, sensors are critical, and adaptability outweighs determinism.

*(7 marks: 4 for matrix, 1 each for the two recommendations, 1 for justification.)*

**(b) Pseudo-Code Pick-and-Place Program (7 marks)**

```
// Block 1: Initialization
CONST robtarget pHome := [[0, 0, 500], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pPickApproach := [[200, 100, 200], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pPick := [[200, 100, 50], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pPlaceApproach := [[400, 200, 200], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
CONST robtarget pPlace := [[400, 200, 50], [1, 0, 0, 0]];
VAR num cycleCount := 0;
VAR num maxCycles := 100;

// Block 2: Sensor / I/O Config
SetDO gripperClose, 0;        // gripper open initially
WaitDI partPresent, 1;        // wait for part sensor

// Block 5 helper: Subroutine
PROC PickAndPlace()
    MoveJ pHome, v1000, fine, tool0;
    MoveJ pPickApproach, v500, fine, tool0;
    MoveL pPick, v100, fine, tool0;
    SetDO gripperClose, 1;     // close gripper
    WaitTime 0.5;
    MoveL pPickApproach, v500, fine, tool0;
    MoveJ pPlaceApproach, v500, fine, tool0;
    MoveL pPlace, v100, fine, tool0;
    SetDO gripperClose, 0;     // open gripper
    WaitTime 0.5;
    MoveL pPlaceApproach, v500, fine, tool0;
ENDPROC

// Block 3 + 4: Main motion + loop
PROC main()
    WHILE cycleCount < maxCycles DO
        PickAndPlace();
        cycleCount := cycleCount + 1;
    ENDWHILE
    MoveJ pHome, v1000, fine, tool0;   // Block 6: termination
ENDPROC
```

**Cycle Time Calculation (4 marks for math + 3 for code):**

**Step 1** — Linear travel distance between pick and place:

$$d = \sqrt{(400-200)^{2} + (200-100)^{2} + 0^{2}} = \sqrt{40000 + 10000} = \sqrt{50000} \approx 223.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2** — Linear motion time at $v = 1$ m/s $= 1000$ mm/s:

$$t_{linear} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{223.6}{1000} \approx 0.224 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3** — Total cycle time (assuming 4 linear moves, 4 joint moves, 2 gripper actions of 0.5 s each):

$$T_{cycle} = 4 \cdot t_{linear} + 4 \cdot t_{joint} + 2 \cdot t_{gripper}$$

Assuming $t_{joint} \approx 0.5$ s each and $t_{gripper} = 0.5$ s:

$$T_{cycle} = 4(0.224) + 4(0.5) + 2(0.5) = 0.896 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.896 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4** — Throughput:

$$\text{Parts/hour} = \dfrac{3600}{3.896} \approx 924 \text{ parts/hour}$$

*(7 marks: 3 for the pseudo-code with all six constructs visible, 4 for the explicit numerical derivation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Three levels** of robot programming: **Joint → Manipulator → Task**.
- ☐ **Five generations** of robot programming languages: **Assembly → Teach → High-Level → OLP → AI/Task-Level**.
- ☐ **Five programming techniques**: **Teach-Pendant, Text-Based, Offline (OLP), Graphical, Task-Level AI**.
- ☐ **Six basic programming constructs**: **Variables, Sequence, Selection, Iteration, Subroutines, Interrupts**.
- ☐ **Six-block program structure**: **Init → I/O Config → Motion → Logic → Tool Action → Termination**.
- ☐ **Online vs Offline** programming: online = on the robot (downtime), offline = in a simulator (no downtime).
- ☐ **Famous languages to memorize**: **VAL-II, AML, RAPT, AL, KAREL, RAPID, KRL, PDL2**.
- ☐ **Famous OLP platforms**: **ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, KUKA.Sim, Delmia, Visual Components**.
- ☐ **Famous modern AI platforms**: **ROS, MoveIt!, CoppeliaSim, PyBullet, NVIDIA Isaac Sim**.
- ☐ **Singularity condition** = $\det(J(q)) = 0$ — *avoid in your waypoints*.
- ☐ **Repeatability formula** = $\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma$ — *industrial arms typically $\pm 0.02$ mm*.
- ☐ **Cycle time formula** = $T_{cycle} = T_{motion} + T_{IO} + T_{wait}$.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 trend**: questions increasingly include **AI / LLM-based task planning** — be ready.
- ☐ Always mention **tool frame, base frame, workobject frame** in Block 1.
- ☐ Always end with **safe home return** in Block 6.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception and Sensor Integration: Types of Sensors (Vision, Rangefinders, IMUs), Sensor Fusion and Data Interpretation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Perception and Sensor Integration: Types of Sensors (Vision, Rangefinders, IMUs), Sensor Fusion and Data Interpretation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Robot Perception?

**Robot Perception** is the process by which a robotic system acquires, interprets, and makes sense of sensory data from its environment to extract meaningful information for decision-making, navigation, and manipulation tasks. It bridges the gap between raw physical signals (light, sound, acceleration, distance) and actionable cognitive models of the world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** *Perception in robotics refers to the systematic acquisition, processing, and interpretation of multi-modal sensory data — using exteroceptive sensors (cameras, rangefinders) and proprioceptive sensors (IMUs, encoders) — to construct an internal representation of the robot's state and its surrounding environment.*

### Conceptual Analogy: The Robot as a Sensory Organism

Imagine a blindfolded person navigating a busy street. They use:
- **A walking cane** (rangefinder) to detect obstacles ahead.
- **Ears** (microphones / ultrasonic sensors) to hear approaching vehicles.
- **The inner ear / vestibular system** (IMU) to know if they are tilting, accelerating, or spinning.
- **A camera held by a guide** (vision sensor) to recognize faces and read signs.

A robot does **exactly this**, but with silicon, optics, and algorithms instead of nerves. **Sensor Fusion** is the brain that combines these diverse inputs — the way your cerebellum merges vision with balance to let you walk in a straight line.

## 1.2 Classification of Robotic Sensors

Robotic sensors are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### A. Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive

| Class | Definition | Examples | Measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Sensors that measure the robot's *own* internal state | IMU, wheel encoders, joint potentiometers, current sensors | Position, velocity, acceleration, orientation, joint angles |
| **Exteroceptive** | Sensors that perceive the *external* environment | Cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, IR, GPS, force/torque | Distance, images, light intensity, force from objects |

### B. Active vs. Passive

| Class | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active** | Emit energy into the environment and measure the response | LiDAR, ultrasonic sonar, radar, structured-light cameras, IR ToF |
| **Passive** | Merely observe naturally occurring energy | Monocular cameras, microphones, thermopiles, magnetometers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module-2 explicitly requires mastery of three major sensor families: **Vision Sensors (Cameras)**, **Rangefinders (LiDAR / Ultrasonic / IR)**, and **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)** — along with the methods to **fuse** their outputs.

## 1.3 The Three Core Sensor Families (Module Overview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Vision Sensors** — Translate reflected light into 2D/3D images using CMOS/CCD arrays. They are *passive exteroceptive* sensors with high information density (millions of pixels per frame).
>
> **2. Rangefinders** — Provide direct geometric measurements of distance. Examples: **LiDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging), **Ultrasonic** sensors, and **Infrared (IR) Time-of-Flight** units. They are *active exteroceptive* sensors.
>
> **3. IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — A *proprioceptive* sensor package combining a **3-axis accelerometer**, a **3-axis gyroscope**, and (often) a **3-axis magnetometer** to estimate orientation, angular velocity, and linear acceleration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vision Sensors — Working Principles

### 2.1.1 Monocular (Single) Camera

A camera approximates the **pinhole model**: light from a 3D world point passes through an infinitesimally small aperture and projects onto a 2D image plane.

**Intrinsic Camera Matrix K:**

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $f_x, f_y$ = focal length in pixel units (horizontally and vertically)
- $c_x, c_y$ = coordinates of the **principal point** (image center)
- $s$ = skew coefficient (usually **0** for modern cameras)

**Projection Equation (World → Image):**

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \cdot [R \mid t] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $[R \mid t]$ is the **extrinsic matrix** (rotation + translation from world frame to camera frame), and $s$ is a scalar (homogeneous coordinate).

### 2.1.2 Stereo Vision

Two cameras separated by a known baseline $B$ compute depth via **triangulation**:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

where $d = x_{left} - x_{right}$ is the **disparity** (pixel difference of the same point in left/right images). Larger disparity = closer object.

### 2.1.3 RGB-D Cameras

Combine a regular RGB sensor with an IR depth sensor (e.g., Microsoft Kinect, Intel RealSense, Apple LiDAR) — providing aligned color + depth per pixel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Camera vs Human Eye Analogy:** A single camera is like one eye (loses depth). A stereo camera = two eyes (binocular disparity gives depth). An RGB-D camera = an eye with a built-in measuring tape.

## 2.2 Rangefinders — Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Ultrasonic Sensors (Sonar)

Emit a high-frequency sound pulse (typically **40 kHz**) and measure the **time-of-flight (ToF)** until the echo returns.

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$

- $v_{sound} \approx 343 \, m/s$ in air at 20°C
- Division by **2** accounts for round-trip travel.
- Typical range: **0.02 m to 4 m**, accuracy $\pm 1 \, cm$.
- **Cone-shaped beam** (~15°–30°) → poor lateral resolution; specular reflections cause errors.

### 2.2.2 LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

Uses pulsed laser (typically **905 nm** or **1550 nm** IR). Measures ToF of photons.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$

- $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ (speed of light)
- Phases: ToF or **Phase-Shift** detection (for short range).
- **2D LiDAR** (e.g., SICK, Hokuyo) — single rotating laser line.
- **3D LiDAR** (e.g., Velodyne VLP-16, 32, 64) — multiple laser diodes spinning on a vertical array.
- Produces **point clouds** with sub-cm accuracy over **100–200 m** range.

### 2.2.3 IR / Time-of-Flight Rangefinders

Emit modulated IR light, measure phase shift or ToF of reflection.
- Short range (**0.1–10 m**), low power, ideal for indoor robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** Many students confuse ultrasonic and LiDAR equations. The *only* difference is the propagation speed ($v_{sound} \approx 343 \, m/s$ vs $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$). Both use the **same ToF formula** $d = v \cdot t / 2$.

## 2.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

A 6-DoF IMU consists of:

| Sub-sensor | Measures | Output | Noise Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accelerometer** | Linear acceleration (specific force) | $a_x, a_y, a_z$ in m/s² | High-frequency noise |
| **Gyroscope** | Angular velocity | $\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z$ in rad/s | Bias drift |
| **Magnetometer** (optional, 9-DoF) | Earth's magnetic field | $B_x, B_y, B_z$ in µT | Hard/soft iron distortion |

### 2.3.1 Accelerometer Model

The accelerometer measures **specific force** (not true acceleration). When stationary, it reads $\approx g$ (gravity) along the up-axis:

$$a_{measured} = a_{true} - g + b_a + n_a$$

where $b_a$ is the bias and $n_a$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise.

### 2.3.2 Gyroscope Model

$$\omega_{measured} = \omega_{true} + b_g + n_g$$

The gyroscope integrates angular velocity to get orientation, but its **bias** causes drift: $\theta(t) = \int \omega \, dt + b_g \cdot t$, leading to orientation errors that grow unboundedly.

### 2.3.3 Tilt Estimation from Accelerometer

When at rest, the gravity vector gives roll ($\phi$) and pitch ($\theta$):

$$\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right), \quad \theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)$$

## 2.4 Sensor Fusion — The Heart of Modern Robotics

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Sensor Fusion** is the algorithmic combination of data from multiple sensors such that the resulting information has **lower uncertainty**, **higher accuracy**, and **greater reliability** than would be possible from any individual sensor.

### 2.4.1 Why Fuse Sensors?

| Limitation | Single Sensor | Fused Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Camera — no scale / no absolute position | Image only | Fuse with IMU for metric scale |
| IMU — drifts over time | Integration error unbounded | Fuse with camera/LiDAR to bound drift |
| LiDAR — sparse at distance / no color | Point cloud only | Fuse with camera for semantic labels |
| GPS — fails indoors | Outages | Fuse with IMU + wheel odometry |

### 2.4.2 Common Fusion Algorithms (Increasing Complexity)

1. **Complementary Filter** — simple weighted sum of low- and high-frequency estimates.
2. **Weighted Average** — combine redundant sensors with known variances.
3. **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — non-linear state estimation.
4. **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** — better for highly non-linear systems.
5. **Particle Filter** — for non-Gaussian noise.
6. **Graph Optimization (Factor Graph SLAM)** — used in VIO, LIO.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the table below — these are the 8–10 equations that recur in KTU Module-2 exam questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole Projection | $s[u,v,1]^T = K[R\vert t][X_w,Y_w,Z_w,1]^T$ | 3D world → 2D image |
| 2 | Stereo Depth | $Z = fB/d$ | Depth from disparity |
| 3 | Ultrasonic ToF | $d = v_{sound} \cdot t / 2$ | Distance via sound echo |
| 4 | LiDAR ToF | $d = c \cdot t / 2$ | Distance via light pulse |
| 5 | Accelerometer Tilt | $\phi = \arctan(a_y/a_z)$ | Roll from gravity |
| 6 | Gyro Integration | $\theta(t) = \int_0^t \omega(\tau) d\tau$ | Orientation from rate |
| 7 | Complementary Filter | $\hat{x} = \alpha \hat{x}_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{acc}$ | Low-cost fusion |
| 8 | KF Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B_k u_k$ | State extrapolation |
| 9 | KF Covariance Update | $P_{k\vert k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\vert k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$ | Uncertainty growth |
| 10 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$ | Trust factor |
| 11 | State Update | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Correction step |
| 12 | Innovation (residual) | $\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}$ | Measurement mismatch |

**Note:** Vertical bars in the table above are rendered using `\vert` (per KTU formatting rule).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pinhole Camera Projection

### Statement
Show that a 3D world point $\mathbf{P}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ projects to a 2D image point $\mathbf{p} = (u, v, 1)^T$ via the pinhole model.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — World to Camera Frame Transformation.**
The camera frame is obtained by rotating by $R$ and translating by $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y, t_z)^T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}_c &= \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{P}_w \\
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* This expresses the camera's pose with respect to the world. The rotation matrix $R$ is orthogonal: $R^T R = I$.

**Step 2 — Perspective Projection (Camera to Image Plane).**
By similar triangles, the pinhole projects $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ onto the image plane at distance $f$ (focal length):

$$x_i = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_i = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

*Logic:* The ratio $X_c / Z_c$ gives the *angle* the incoming ray makes with the optical axis; multiplying by $f$ gives the image-plane distance.

**Step 3 — Convert to Pixel Coordinates.**
Image-plane coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ are mapped to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ using the intrinsic matrix $K$ (with pixel scaling $\alpha, \beta$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$):

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= \alpha \cdot x_i + c_x = \alpha f \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \\
v &= \beta \cdot y_i + c_y = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final Homogeneous Form.**
Substituting $f_x = \alpha f$ and $f_y = \beta f$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

Combining extrinsic and intrinsic parts:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LiDAR / Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Range

### Statement
Derive the range equation for a pulsed active rangefinder (LiDAR or ultrasonic).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define variables.** Let $d$ = range to target, $v$ = propagation speed of the medium, $t_{ToF}$ = measured time between pulse emission and echo detection.

**Step 2 — Round-trip distance.** The pulse travels from sensor → target → sensor, covering $2d$:

$$2d = v \cdot t_{ToF}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for range.**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in constants for LiDAR (in air, $v = c$):**

$$d_{LiDAR} = \frac{c \cdot t_{ToF}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot t_{ToF}}{2} \, m$$

**Step 5 — Plug in constants for ultrasonic (in air at 20°C, $v = 343 \, m/s$):**

$$d_{US} = \frac{343 \cdot t_{ToF}}{2} \, m$$

**Numerical Example:** A LiDAR receives a return pulse after $t = 333 \, ns$. Compute the range.

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \, m/s \times 333 \times 10^{-9} \, s}{2} \\
&= \frac{99.9}{2} = 49.95 \, m \approx 50 \, m
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Complementary Filter for IMU Fusion

### Statement
Derive a 1-D complementary filter that fuses a high-drift gyroscope with a noisy-but-stable accelerometer tilt estimate.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Gyroscope tilt update** (integrate angular rate $\omega$ over sampling period $T$):

$$\theta_{gyro}(k) = \theta(k-1) + \omega \cdot T$$

*Logic:* Accurate in the short term; drifts over time due to bias.

**Step 2 — Accelerometer tilt** (from gravity vector, valid only at quasi-static):

$$\theta_{acc}(k) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$$

*Logic:* Long-term accurate (no drift) but noisy and invalid during motion.

**Step 3 — Complementary Filter Combination.**
Define weighting $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. At low frequencies, trust $\theta_{acc}$; at high frequencies, trust $\theta_{gyro}$:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \alpha \cdot \hat{\theta}(k-1) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{acc}(k) + \beta \cdot \omega(k) \cdot T$$

A common simplified form:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{acc}(k) + \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \omega \cdot T)$$

*Logic:* The high-pass gyroscope term cancels the low-pass accelerometer noise. Typically $\alpha \approx 0.95$–$0.98$.

**Step 4 — Transfer function interpretation.**
In the $s$-domain, this is a first-order complementary filter:

$$\hat{\Theta}(s) = \frac{s}{s + \omega_c} \cdot \frac{\Omega(s)}{s} + \frac{\omega_c}{s + \omega_c} \cdot \Theta_{acc}(s)$$

where $\omega_c = (1-\alpha)/T$ is the cutoff frequency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Kalman Filter — Prediction & Update Steps

### Statement
Derive the recursive equations of the Linear Kalman Filter from the Bayesian estimation framework.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — State-space model.** Linear discrete system:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_k &= F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k) \\
z_k &= H_k x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* $F_k$ propagates state, $H_k$ projects state to measurement, $Q_k, R_k$ are process/measurement noise covariances.

**Step 2 — Time-update (Prediction).**
Project the state estimate forward:

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B_k u_k$$

*Logic:* The best guess for $x_k$ before seeing $z_k$ is the previous estimate propagated by the dynamics.

Project the uncertainty (covariance):

$$P_{k \vert k-1} = F_k P_{k-1 \vert k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

*Logic:* Covariance grows due to the linearization $F$ and additive process noise $Q$.

**Step 3 — Compute innovation (residual).**
Difference between actual and predicted measurement:

$$\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$$

*Logic:* This is the "surprise" — how wrong our prediction was.

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance.**

$$S_k = H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k$$

*Logic:* Combines our prediction's uncertainty with the sensor's noise.

**Step 5 — Kalman gain.**

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T S_k^{-1}$$

*Logic:* Optimal weighting. If $R_k \to 0$ (sensor perfect), $K_k \to H_k^{-1}$ → trust measurement fully. If $P_{k\vert k-1} \to 0$ (we are certain), $K_k \to 0$ → ignore measurement.

**Step 6 — Measurement update (Correction).**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k$$

**Step 7 — Covariance update.**

$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

*Logic:* Posterior covariance is always ≤ prior covariance — the filter always *reduces* uncertainty (or holds it constant).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Sensor Family | Working Principle | Output | Strengths | Weaknesses | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Monocular Camera** | Light → CMOS/CCD | 2D image (RGB) | Rich color, semantic info, low cost | No depth, scale ambiguity, light-sensitive | Object recognition, SLAM |
| **Stereo Camera** | Triangulation | 2D + depth | Depth without active emission | Computationally heavy, fails on textureless surfaces | Outdoor navigation, ADAS |
| **RGB-D Camera** | IR ToF / Structured light | 2D + depth | Direct depth, indoor robust | Limited range (~5 m), sunlight interference | Indoor mapping, pick-and-place |
| **Ultrasonic** | Sound pulse ToF | 1D distance | Cheap, robust, weather-proof | Wide beam, specular errors, slow | Obstacle avoidance, parking |
| **LiDAR (2D/3D)** | Laser ToF | Point cloud | Long range, geometric accuracy | Expensive, no color, moving parts | Autonomous cars, mapping |
| **IMU (6-/9-DoF)** | MEMS accelerometer + gyro + magnetometer | Acceleration, angular rate, heading | High rate, no external dep., small | Drift, no absolute reference | Pose estimation, stabilization |
| **GPS / GNSS** | Satellite ToF | Lat/Lon/Alt | Global absolute position | Outdoor only, ~m accuracy | Outdoor robot localization |

## 4.2 Sensor Fusion Flow (Kalman Filter Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[Previous State x_prev]:::kfNode
    covA[Previous Covariance P_prev]:::kfNode
    predA[State Prediction x_pred]:::kfNode
    predB[Covariance Prediction P_pred]:::kfNode
    measA[Measurement z_k]:::kfNode
    innovA[Innovation y_tilde]:::kfNode
    gainA[Kalman Gain K]:::kfNode
    updA[State Update x_new]:::kfNode
    updB[Covariance Update P_new]:::kfNode
    outA[Output Estimate]:::kfNode

    stateA --> predA
    covA --> predB
    predA --> innovA
    measA --> innovA
    predB --> gainA
    predA --> gainA
    innovA --> updA
    gainA --> updA
    gainA --> updB
    predB --> updB
    updA --> outA
    updB --> outA
    updA -.feedback loop.-> stateA
    updB -.feedback loop.-> covA

    classDef kfNode fill:#C6E2FF,stroke:#036,color:#000
```
