# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Sensor Fusion." Why is it essential in modern robotic systems? Name two fusion algorithms used in practice.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** *Sensor fusion is the process of combining data from multiple sensors such that the resulting estimate is more accurate, complete, and reliable than that of any individual sensor. It exploits the complementary strengths and statistical independence of different sensing modalities.*
>
> **Essentiality (1 mark):** No single sensor is perfect. Cameras lack metric scale, IMUs drift, LiDARs lack color, GPS fails indoors. Fusion overcomes these limitations and provides **redundancy** (fault tolerance) and **complementarity** (each sensor covers the others' blind spots).
>
> **Two algorithms (0 marks, supplementary):** Complementary Filter, Kalman Filter, EKF, Particle Filter, Graph Optimization (Factor Graph).

### Q2. With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a pinhole camera. Write its projection equation.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> A pinhole camera has an infinitesimally small aperture through which light from a 3D scene passes and projects onto a 2D image plane. Each world point $(X, Y, Z)$ maps to image point $(x, y) = (fX/Z, fY/Z)$ where $f$ is the focal length.
>
> **Projection Equation:** $s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, where $K$ is the intrinsic matrix and $[R \mid t]$ is the extrinsic matrix.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Vision + Rangefinders (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — Stereo Vision Depth Computation (7 Marks)
Explain the principle of stereo vision. Two cameras with a baseline of 12 cm and focal length 800 pixels capture a feature point with disparity 6 pixels. Compute the depth of the point. What is the minimum disparity if the maximum measurable depth is 25 m?

**Model Solution:**

> **Principle (3 marks):** Stereo vision uses two cameras separated by a known baseline $B$. The same world point appears at different pixel locations in the two images; this horizontal pixel difference is the **disparity** $d$. Using similar triangles, depth is computed as $Z = fB/d$.
>
> **Depth Calculation (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \, px \times 0.12 \, m}{6 \, px} \\
> &= \frac{96}{6} = 16 \, m
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Minimum Disparity (2 marks):** Minimum disparity corresponds to maximum depth:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> d_{min} &= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z_{max}} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{25} \\
> &= \frac{96}{25} = 3.84 \, px
> \end{aligned}
> $$

#### Part (b) — LiDAR and Ultrasonic Comparison (7 Marks)
With necessary equations, explain the working of (i) ultrasonic sensor and (ii) LiDAR. A LiDAR mounted on a mobile robot detects an obstacle at 8 m. What is the time-of-flight of the laser pulse? ($c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$)

**Model Solution:**

> **Ultrasonic (3 marks):** An ultrasonic sensor emits a high-frequency sound pulse (typically 40 kHz) which travels through air, reflects off an obstacle, and returns to the sensor. The Time-of-Flight $t$ is measured, and the distance is computed as $d = v_{sound} \cdot t / 2$ with $v_{sound} \approx 343 \, m/s$. The factor ½ accounts for round-trip travel. Typical range: 2 cm to 4 m. Wide beam (~15°–30°) limits lateral resolution.
>
> **LiDAR (2 marks):** LiDAR emits a narrow laser pulse (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm wavelength). The reflected pulse is detected by a photodiode, and the round-trip time gives the range as $d = c \cdot t / 2$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$. 2D LiDAR has a single rotating laser; 3D LiDAR uses an array of lasers to produce dense point clouds.
>
> **Numerical (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> t_{ToF} &= \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 8}{3 \times 10^8} \\
> &= \frac{16}{3 \times 10^8} = 5.33 \times 10^{-8} \, s = 53.3 \, ns
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

### Question B: IMU + Sensor Fusion (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — IMU Working Principle (7 Marks)
Explain the construction and working of a 6-DoF Inertial Measurement Unit. With suitable mathematical models, derive the expressions for tilt estimation using an accelerometer.

**Model Solution:**

> **Construction (3 marks):** A 6-DoF IMU integrates **three MEMS accelerometers** (one per axis $x, y, z$) and **three MEMS gyroscopes** (one per axis) on a single chip. A 9-DoF IMU additionally includes a 3-axis magnetometer.
>
> - **Accelerometer:** Detects proof-mass displacement caused by inertial force, converted to a voltage via capacitive sensing.
> - **Gyroscope:** Detects Coriolis force on a vibrating proof mass, producing a voltage proportional to angular rate.
>
> **Mathematical Model (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> a_{meas} &= a_{true} - g + b_a + n_a \\
> \omega_{meas} &= \omega_{true} + b_g + n_g
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Tilt Estimation (2 marks):** When the robot is stationary, the only acceleration is gravity $g$ along the body $z$-axis. The components $a_x, a_y$ give roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \phi &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right) \\
> \theta &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)
> \end{aligned}
> $$

#### Part (b) — Kalman Filter-Based Sensor Fusion (7 Marks)
What is sensor fusion? With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the prediction and update equations of a Kalman Filter used to fuse IMU and camera data for Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO).

**Model Solution:**

> **Definition (1 mark):** *Sensor fusion is the algorithmic combination of data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and complete estimate than any individual sensor could provide.*
>
> **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Kalman-Filter loop diagram in Section 4.2. It shows the prediction-correction cycle.
>
> **State-Space Model (1 mark):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> x_k &= F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k) \\
> z_k &= H_k x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> Here $x$ may include position, velocity, orientation, IMU biases. $z$ contains both IMU measurements ($\omega, a$) and visual features.
>
> **Prediction Step (1 mark):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} &= F_k \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B_k u_k \\
> P_{k \vert k-1} &= F_k P_{k-1 \vert k-1} F_k^T + Q_k
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Update Step (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \tilde{y}_k &= z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} \\
> K_k &= P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1} \\
> \hat{x}_{k \vert k} &= \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k \\
> P_{k \vert k} &= (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> The **prediction** uses the IMU's high-rate integration; the **update** corrects drift using camera observations of feature points (e.g., ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ToF equations ($d = vt/2$, *not* $d = vt$). Examiners specifically look for this — 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Confusing the speed of sound ($343 \, m/s$) with speed of light ($3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$).** Mention the medium explicitly.
> 3. **In Kalman filter derivations**, skipping the innovation covariance $S_k$ step. Full credit requires **all 7 equations** in sequence.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the units** of every quantity in numerical problems. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for each missing unit.
> 5. **In camera projection problems**, students often write $K \cdot P$ directly without the extrinsic matrix $[R \mid t]$. The full transformation requires both.
> 6. **Confusing complementary filter with Kalman filter.** Complementary filter is a *single-scalar* heuristic; Kalman filter is an *optimal Bayesian estimator* with covariance tracking.
> 7. **Pinhole derivation**: always derive $x_i = fX_c/Z_c$ using **similar triangles** explicitly, not just state the result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2**

### A. Vision Sensors
- Pinhole model: $s[u,v,1]^T = K[R \vert t][X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$
- $K$ = intrinsic matrix (focal length, principal point); $[R \vert t]$ = extrinsic (pose).
- Stereo depth: $Z = fB/d$, where $d$ is disparity and $B$ is baseline.
- **Limitations of monocular vision:** no metric scale, no absolute depth.

### B. Rangefinders
- **Ultrasonic:** $d = 343 \cdot t / 2$, range 0.02–4 m, wide beam, slow.
- **LiDAR:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$, range up to 200 m, narrow beam, point cloud.
- **IR ToF:** short range, low power, indoor use.
- All active sensors use **ToF principle** with the same $d = vt/2$ form.

### C. IMU
- 6-DoF = 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope.
- 9-DoF = + 3-axis magnetometer.
- Accelerometer measures specific force → static tilt (roll/pitch from gravity).
- Gyroscope integrates angular rate → drifts over time.
- Magnetometer gives absolute heading → disturbs near ferrous materials.

### D. Sensor Fusion
- **Complementary filter:** $ \hat{\theta} = \alpha \hat{\theta}_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \hat{\theta}_{acc} $.
- **Kalman filter:** optimal linear estimator; 2-step (predict, update).
- **EKF / UKF:** extensions for non-linear systems.
- **Practical systems:** VINS-Mono, ORB-SLAM3, LOAM, Cartographer, Robot Operating System (ROS) `robot_localization` package.

### E. Exam Essentials
- Memorize the **8 core equations** (Section 2.5 table).
- Know the **classification tree** of sensors (Section 4.3).
- Be able to **derive** the Kalman filter equations and the pinhole projection.
- For numerical problems, **always include units and state assumptions** (e.g., $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$, $v_{sound} = 343 \, m/s$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Planning: Path Planning Algorithms (A*, PRM), Motion Planning in Dynamic Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Planning: Path Planning Algorithms (A*, PRM), Motion Planning in Dynamic Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Motion Planning — The Foundational Definition

**Motion Planning** is the computational problem of finding a continuous sequence of valid robot configurations that moves a robot from an initial configuration to a goal configuration while avoiding collisions with obstacles in the workspace.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, motion planning is decomposed into two hierarchical sub-problems:

1. **Path Planning** — A purely *geometric* problem: "Find a sequence of points in the configuration space (C-space) that connects start to goal and is collision-free." The notion of *time* is absent.
2. **Trajectory Planning** — A *temporal* problem: "Parameterize the geometric path with a time law (velocity and acceleration profiles) so that the robot can physically execute it."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Space (C-Space) — KTU Exam Favourite**
> The set of *all possible* poses (position + orientation) a robot can attain. A rigid robot in 2D lives in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (3-DOF). A 6-DOF industrial arm lives in $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ (6-DOF). The robot itself is reduced to a *point* in C-space, and obstacles are "grown" by the robot's swept volume — this transformation is called the **Minkowski Sum**.

### 1.2 The Two Algorithmic Giants — A* and PRM

**A\* (A-Star) Search** is a *graph-search best-first algorithm* that uses a heuristic to estimate the cost from any node to the goal. It is **complete** (finds a path if one exists) and **optimal** (finds the lowest-cost path) when the heuristic is *admissible*.

**PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap Method)** is a *sampling-based multi-query planner* that randomly scatters "milestone" nodes in free C-space, connects them with collision-free local paths, and stores the resulting graph as a roadmap for rapid future queries.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Navigator

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a room full of furniture. **A\*** is like feeling the wall, then *systematically* walking toward the door while estimating how far the door *might* be using sound. You explore methodically, never backtracking unnecessarily.
>
> **PRM** is like first sending a swarm of small drones to randomly photograph the entire room, building a *map of safe stepping stones*, and then simply *looking up* the shortest route on this map every time someone asks "how do I get to the door?" The drones are the *learning phase*; the lookup is the *query phase*.

### 1.4 Dynamic Environments — When the World Moves

A **dynamic environment** contains moving obstacles (e.g., walking pedestrians, conveyor belts, other robots). The path is no longer static; the planner must *re-plan in real time* as the world evolves. Standard algorithms used here are **D\* Lite**, **Artificial Potential Fields (APF)**, and **Velocity Obstacles (VO)**.

### 1.5 Key Terminology — Board-Exam Standard

- **g(n)**: Actual cost from start to node *n*.
- **h(n)**: Heuristic estimated cost from node *n* to goal.
- **f(n) = g(n) + h(n)**: Total estimated cost through node *n*.
- **Admissible Heuristic**: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$, i.e., it never *overestimates* true cost.
- **Consistent (Monotonic) Heuristic**: $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$.
- **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$)**: Subset of C-space with no collision.
- **Roadmap**: Graph $G = (V, E)$ of collision-free milestones and edges.
- **Re-planning**: Online regeneration of path when environment changes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 A* Search — Operational Decomposition

The A* algorithm maintains two lists and one scoring function. It is the **gold standard for discrete grid-based path planning**.

**Operational Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Initialization**: Insert the start node $s$ into the **OPEN list** (priority queue). Set $g(s) = 0$ and $f(s) = h(s)$. Initialize **CLOSED list** as empty.
- **Step 2 — Termination Test**: If OPEN is empty → return FAILURE. If the node with the lowest $f$ in OPEN is the goal → reconstruct path via parent pointers and return SUCCESS.
- **Step 3 — Expansion**: Pop the lowest-$f$ node $n$ from OPEN, move it to CLOSED.
- **Step 4 — Successor Generation**: For every neighbor $n'$ of $n$:
  - Compute tentative $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
  - If $n'$ is in CLOSED and $g(n') \ge$ tentative cost → skip.
  - If $n'$ is in OPEN and $g(n') \le$ tentative cost → skip.
  - Otherwise: update $g(n')$, set parent to $n$, compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$, push to OPEN.
- **Step 5 — Loop**: Return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind A\*'s Optimality**
> Any node popped from OPEN has the *lowest possible* $f$-value among all unexpanded frontier nodes. If $h$ is admissible, the first time the goal is popped, no other path can possibly be cheaper. This is the formal proof KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.2 Common Heuristics — KTU Cheat Sheet

For a 4-connected or 8-connected grid with cell side length $d$:

- **Manhattan Distance** (4-conn admissible):
$$h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) = d \cdot \left( \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert \right)$$

- **Euclidean Distance** (8-conn admissible):
$$h_{\text{Euclid}}(n) = d \cdot \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$$

- **Chebyshev Distance** (8-conn, kings-move admissible):
$$h_{\text{Cheb}}(n) = d \cdot \max\left( \vert x_n - x_g \vert, \vert y_n - y_g \vert \right)$$

- **Diagonal Distance** (admissible for 8-conn with diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}\,d$):
$$h_{\text{diag}}(n) = d \cdot \left( \left( \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert \right) - (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min\left( \vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert \right) \right)$$

### 2.3 PRM — Two-Phase Architecture

**Phase 1 — Learning (Construction):**
- Sample $N$ random configurations $q_i \in \mathcal{C}$.
- Discard any $q_i$ that lies in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (collision check).
- For each $q_i$, find the $k$ nearest free neighbors within radius $r$.
- For each neighbor pair, run a *local planner* (typically straight-line) and add the edge to $G$ if collision-free.

**Phase 2 — Query:**
- Connect $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ to the roadmap.
- Run a graph search (e.g., Dijkstra or A*) on $G$.
- Smooth the path if needed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Motion Planning Core

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| A* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Cost units (m, s, energy) | Real-time mobile-robot navigation |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Dimensionless | Guarantees optimality |
| Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Cost units | Guarantees no re-expansion |
| PRM success probability | $P_{\text{conn}} = 1 - (1 - p)^{\alpha N}$ | Probability | Determines $N$ for roadmap completeness |
| Minkowski Obstacle | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-R)$ | C-space region | Collision checking reduction |
| Potential Field force | $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}$ | Newtons | Reactive obstacle avoidance |
| Dynamic window | $v \in [v_a, v_b], \omega \in [\omega_a, \omega_b]$ | m/s, rad/s | Constrained real-time planning |
| Velocity Obstacle cone | $VO = \{ \mathbf{v} \mid \exists t, \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{v}t \in O_{\text{moving}} \}$ | Velocity set | Multi-agent collision avoidance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Where These Algorithms Are Deployed**
> - **A\***: Google Maps road routing, warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva), Mars rover path planning (NASA).
> - **PRM**: Industrial manipulator offline programming, humanoid footstep planning.
> - **APF / VO**: Real-time drone swarms, autonomous cars in traffic, service robots in malls.

### 2.5 Dynamic Environment Planners — Quick Comparison

| Algorithm | Re-planning Cost | Completeness | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D\* Lite** | Incremental (reuses prior search) | Complete | Known map, changing obstacles |
| **Artificial Potential Field** | O(1) per step | Not complete (local minima) | Fast reactive avoidance |
| **Velocity Obstacles** | O(n) per step | Complete in velocity space | Multi-agent dynamic crowds |
| **RRT\*** | Re-roots from scratch | Probabilistically complete | High-DOF, dynamic C-space |
| **MPC-based** | Solves QP at each step | Constraint-satisfying | Robot arms with dynamics |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — A* on a 4x4 Grid

**Problem Setup:**
- 4×4 grid, 4-connected, each move cost = **1**.
- Start $S = (0,0)$, Goal $G = (3,3)$.
- Heuristic $h(n) = $ Manhattan distance.
- Obstacles at $(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)$.

**Initial State:**
- $g(S) = 0$, $h(S) = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 0-3 \vert = 6$, so $f(S) = 6$.
- OPEN = {S(0,0)}; CLOSED = {}.

**Iteration 1 — Pop S(0,0):**
Neighbors: (1,0), (0,1).
- (1,0): $g = 0 + 1 = 1$, $h = \vert 1-3 \vert + \vert 0-3 \vert = 5$, $f = 6$.
- (0,1): $g = 1$, $h = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 1-3 \vert = 5$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(1,0)[6], (0,1)[6]}; CLOSED = {(0,0)}.

**Iteration 2 — Pop (1,0) (ties broken arbitrarily):**
Neighbors: (0,0) [skip, in CLOSED], (2,0), (1,1) [obstacle, skip].
- (2,0): $g = 2$, $h = \vert 2-3 \vert + \vert 0-3 \vert = 4$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(0,1)[6], (2,0)[6]}; CLOSED = {(0,0),(1,0)}.

**Iteration 3 — Pop (0,1):**
Neighbors: (0,0) [skip], (0,2), (1,1) [obstacle, skip].
- (0,2): $g = 2$, $h = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 2-3 \vert = 4$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(2,0)[6], (0,2)[6]}; CLOSED = add (0,1).

**Iteration 4 — Pop (2,0):**
Neighbors: (1,0) [CLOSED], (3,0), (2,1) [obstacle].
- (3,0): $g = 3$, $h = \vert 3-3 \vert + \vert 0-3 \vert = 3$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(0,2)[6], (3,0)[6]}; CLOSED = add (2,0).

**Iteration 5 — Pop (0,2):**
Neighbors: (0,1) [CLOSED], (0,3), (1,2) [obstacle].
- (0,3): $g = 3$, $h = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 3-3 \vert = 3$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(3,0)[6], (0,3)[6]}; CLOSED = add (0,2).

**Iteration 6 — Pop (3,0):**
Neighbors: (2,0) [CLOSED], (3,1).
- (3,1): $g = 4$, $h = \vert 3-3 \vert + \vert 1-3 \vert = 2$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(0,3)[6], (3,1)[6]}; CLOSED = add (3,0).

**Iteration 7 — Pop (0,3):**
Neighbors: (0,2) [CLOSED], (1,3).
- (1,3): $g = 4$, $h = \vert 1-3 \vert + \vert 3-3 \vert = 2$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(3,1)[6], (1,3)[6]}; CLOSED = add (0,3).

**Iteration 8 — Pop (1,3):**
Neighbors: (0,3) [CLOSED], (2,3), (1,2) [obstacle].
- (2,3): $g = 5$, $h = \vert 2-3 \vert + \vert 3-3 \vert = 1$, $f = 6$.
- OPEN = {(3,1)[6], (2,3)[6]}; CLOSED = add (1,3).

**Iteration 9 — Pop (2,3):**
Neighbors: (1,3) [CLOSED], (3,3), (2,2).
- **(3,3) = GOAL**: $g = 6$, $h = 0$, $f = 6$.
- (2,2): $g = 6$, $h = \vert 2-3 \vert + \vert 2-3 \vert = 2$, $f = 8$.

**Termination:** Pop next node (3,1) or (3,3) — if (3,3) is the lowest, **A\* terminates with optimal path**.

**Optimal Path Reconstruction:** $S \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)$ — though we need to verify (3,2) is free. Total $g(\text{goal}) = 6$.

### 3.2 Admissibility Proof of Manhattan Heuristic — Sketch

**Claim:** $h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) \le h^*(n)$ for a 4-connected grid with unit step cost.

**Proof:**

Assume the goal is at $(x_g, y_g)$ and current node is at $(x_n, y_n)$. Define the offset:

$$\Delta x = \vert x_n - x_g \vert, \quad \Delta y = \vert y_n - y_g \vert$$

Any 4-connected path from $n$ to $G$ must make at least $\Delta x$ horizontal moves and $\Delta y$ vertical moves. Since each move has cost 1:

$$h^*(n) \ge \Delta x + \Delta y = h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n)$$

$$\therefore h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) \le h^*(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 PRM Connectivity Probability — Derivation

Suppose a single local connection succeeds with probability $p$. We sample $N$ nodes, and each node on average has $\alpha$ neighbors in the graph. The probability that **none** of the $\alpha$ edges from a node succeed is:

$$P_{\text{isolated}} = (1 - p)^{\alpha}$$

Probability that a node is **not** isolated (i.e., has at least one successful edge):

$$P_{\text{conn, single}} = 1 - (1-p)^{\alpha}$$

For the *whole roadmap* to be connected with probability $P_{\text{roadmap}}$:

$$P_{\text{roadmap}} \approx \left( 1 - (1-p)^{\alpha} \right)^N$$

Solving for $N$ given a desired confidence $P_{\text{roadmap}}$:

$$N \ge \frac{\ln(1 - P_{\text{roadmap}})}{\ln\left( 1 - (1-p)^{\alpha} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**
> This is why PRM "works" — even though it samples randomly, by the *Law of Large Numbers* a sufficient $N$ guarantees a connected roadmap with overwhelming probability, *without enumerating the entire space*. This is the central advantage in high-dimensional C-spaces where grids are infeasible.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| 2D warehouse, static map | A* with Manhattan | Discrete, low-DOF, optimal path required |
| 6-DOF arm, static | PRM + smoothing | High C-space, multi-query usage |
| 7-DOF humanoid walking | RRT* | Narrow passages, differential constraints |
| Mobile robot, moving people | D* Lite + APF | Incremental re-plan + reactive safety |
| Drone swarm in air | ORCA / VO | Velocity-space, decentralized, real-time |
| Self-driving car | Hybrid A* + MPC | Continuous state, kinodynamic constraints |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 A* Search Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START"] --> B["Initialize OPEN with start node s"]
    B --> C["Set g(s) = 0, f(s) = h(s)"]
    C --> D{"OPEN empty?"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["Return FAILURE"]
    D -- "NO" --> F["Pop node n with lowest f(n)"]
    F --> G{"n equals GOAL?"}
    G -- "YES" --> H["Reconstruct path via parents"]
    H --> I["Return SUCCESS"]
    G -- "NO" --> J["Move n to CLOSED"]
    J --> K["For each neighbor n prime of n"]
    K --> L["Compute tentative g = g(n) + c(n, n prime)"]
    L --> M{"n prime in CLOSED and g not improved?"}
    M -- "YES" --> K
    M -- "NO" --> N{"n prime in OPEN and g not improved?"}
    N -- "YES" --> K
    N -- "NO" --> O["Update g(n prime) and parent"]
    O --> P["Compute f(n prime) = g + h"]
    P --> Q["Insert n prime into OPEN"]
    Q --> K
    K --> R{"All neighbors processed?"}
    R -- "YES" --> D
    R -- "NO" --> K
```

### 4.2 PRM Two-Phase Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1 - LEARNING OFFLINE"]
        A1["Random Sampling of N configs q_i"] --> A2{"Collision Check"}
        A2 -- "Free" --> A3["Add to milestone set V"]
        A2 -- "Obstacle" --> A1
        A3 --> A4["Find k nearest free neighbors"]
        A4 --> A5["Local Planner edge test"]
        A5 -- "Free edge" --> A6["Add edge to roadmap E"]
        A5 -- "Blocked" --> A4
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2 - QUERY ONLINE"]
        B1["New query start q_s, goal q_g"] --> B2["Connect to roadmap V"]
        B2 --> B3["Run A star on graph G"]
        B3 --> B4["Extract geometric path"]
        B4 --> B5["Path smoothing spline"]
        B5 --> B6["Return executable trajectory"]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
```

### 4.3 Dynamic Environment Re-Planning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING["SENSING LAYER"]
        S1["LIDAR point cloud"]
        S2["Camera obstacle detection"]
        S3["IMU odometry"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        P1["Dynamic obstacle tracker"]
        P2["Predict trajectories Kalman"]
    end

    subgraph PLAN["PLANNING LAYER"]
        Q1["D star Lite global re-planner"]
        Q2["Artificial Potential Field local"]
        Q3["Velocity Obstacle check"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        R1["Trajectory tracker"]
        R2["Motor commands"]
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> SENSING
```

### 4.4 Heuristic Quality vs Nodes Expanded

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1["Heuristic h1 admissible loose"] --> H2["Expands MANY nodes"]
    H3["Heuristic h2 admissible tight"] --> H4["Expands FEWER nodes"]
    H5["Heuristic h3 inadmissible"] --> H6["Fastest but NOT optimal"]
    H1 --> H7["Example Manhattan 4 conn"]
    H3 --> H8["Example Euclidean 8 conn"]
    H5 --> H9["Example scaled h = 2 times Manhattan"]
```

### 4.5 Concept Map — Module 3 Motion Planning Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP["Motion Planning"] --> P1["Path Planning - geometric"]
    TOP --> P2["Trajectory Planning - temporal"]
    P1 --> A1["Search Based - A star"]
    P1 --> A2["Sampling Based - PRM, RRT"]
    P1 --> A3["Optimization Based - CHOMP, TrajOpt"]
    A1 --> A4["Static environments"]
    A2 --> A5["High DOF, complex geometry"]
    P2 --> T1["Dynamic environments"]
    T1 --> T2["D star Lite incremental"]
    T1 --> T3["Reactive APF and VO"]
    T1 --> T4["Predictive MPC"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Path Planning and Trajectory Planning in robotics. Give one example algorithm for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Path Planning (1 mark):** A purely *geometric* problem that finds a collision-free sequence of configurations from start to goal in C-space. Time and dynamics are ignored. **Example:** A* algorithm on a grid.
- **Trajectory Planning (1 mark):** A *temporal* problem that parameterizes the geometric path with velocity, acceleration, and time profiles respecting robot dynamic constraints. **Example:** Time-optimal trajectory generation using cubic splines.
- **Key Distinction (1 mark):** Path = "Where to go"; Trajectory = "When and how fast to go along the path."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*State the conditions for a heuristic function to be admissible and consistent. Why is consistency preferred in A* implementation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Admissibility (1 mark):** A heuristic $h(n)$ is *admissible* if for every node $n$, $h(n) \le h^*(n)$, where $h^*$ is the true minimum cost to goal. It never overestimates.
- **Consistency (1 mark):** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$. Consistency implies admissibility for a positive-cost graph.
- **Why Preferred (1 mark):** A consistent heuristic guarantees that once a node is expanded (CLOSED), its $g$-value is optimal and need never be re-opened, saving computation. It also ensures the $f$-values along any path are non-decreasing.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — A* Algorithm in Depth**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the A* algorithm with its evaluation function. State and prove the admissibility condition. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a 5×5 grid with the following: Start $S = (0,0)$, Goal $G = (4,4)$, obstacles at $(2,2), (2,3), (3,2)$. Use 4-connected motion with unit cost. Use Manhattan distance heuristic. Compute the $f$-value for every expanded node and trace the optimal path. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (4 marks):** A* is a best-first graph search that combines the *actual* cost-so-far $g(n)$ with a *heuristic estimate* $h(n)$ via the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. It maintains an OPEN priority queue (sorted by $f$) and a CLOSED set of expanded nodes. At each iteration, the node with the lowest $f$ in OPEN is expanded. (Algorithm steps as per Section 2.1 above.)

**Admissibility Proof (3 marks):**

*Claim:* A* returns an optimal path if $h$ is admissible.

*Proof Sketch:* Let $C^*$ be the optimal goal cost. Suppose A* terminates at a non-optimal goal $n$ with $f(n) = g(n) > C^*$. At termination, OPEN contains only nodes with $f \ge f(n) > C^*$. For any unexpanded node $m$ in OPEN, $f(m) = g(m) + h(m) \ge g(m)$. But the true cost of any path through $m$ is $\ge g(m) + h^*(m) \ge g(m) + h(m)$ (since $h$ is admissible). So all paths through OPEN nodes have cost $> C^*$, contradicting the existence of the optimal path. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Grid Solution (7 marks):** Following the same methodology as Section 3.1:

| Step | Node Expanded | g | h (Manhattan) | f | OPEN after step |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0,0) | 0 | 8 | 8 | (1,0), (0,1) |
| 2 | (1,0) | 1 | 7 | 8 | (0,1), (2,0) |
| 3 | (0,1) | 1 | 7 | 8 | (2,0), (0,2) |
| 4 | (2,0) | 2 | 6 | 8 | (0,2), (3,0) |
| 5 | (0,2) | 2 | 6 | 8 | (3,0), (0,3) |
| 6 | (3,0) | 3 | 5 | 8 | (0,3), (4,0), (3,1) |
| 7 | (0,3) | 3 | 5 | 8 | (4,0), (3,1), (0,4) |
| 8 | (4,0) | 4 | 4 | 8 | (3,1), (0,4), (4,1) |
| 9 | (3,1) | 4 | 4 | 8 | (0,4), (4,1), (3,2)→obs |
| 10 | (0,4) | 4 | 4 | 8 | (4,1), (1,4) |
| 11 | (4,1) | 5 | 3 | 8 | (1,4), (4,2) |
| 12 | (1,4) | 5 | 3 | 8 | (4,2), (2,4), (1,3) |
| 13 | (4,2) | 6 | 2 | 8 | (2,4), (1,3), (4,3) |
| 14 | (2,4) | 6 | 2 | 8 | (1,3), (4,3), (2,3)→obs |
| 15 | (1,3) | 6 | 2 | 8 | (4,3), (1,2) |
| 16 | (4,3) | 7 | 1 | 8 | (1,2), (4,4) |
| 17 | (1,2) | 7 | 1 | 8 | (4,4), (1,1) |
| 18 | (4,4) **GOAL** | 8 | 0 | 8 | — |

**Optimal Path:** $S(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4) = G$.
**Total Path Cost:** $g(G) = 8$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — PRM and Dynamic Environments**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Probabilistic Roadmap Method (PRM) with its two phases. Discuss its completeness and the conditions under which it is probabilistically complete. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare static path planning with planning in dynamic environments. Explain any two algorithms used for motion planning in dynamic environments with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PRM Explanation (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** PRM is a *sampling-based* multi-query motion planner introduced by Kavraki et al. (1996) for high-dimensional configuration spaces.

**Phase 1 — Learning/Construction (3 marks):**
- Sample $N$ configurations uniformly at random from C-space.
- Discard samples in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (collision-checked using the robot's swept volume / Minkowski sum).
- For each free milestone $q_i$, find the $k$ nearest neighbors (or all within radius $r$).
- For each neighbor pair $(q_i, q_j)$, use a *local planner* (usually straight-line) to verify edge is collision-free; if so, add to $E$.

**Phase 2 — Query (2 marks):**
- Connect $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ to the roadmap $G = (V, E)$.
- Run A* or Dijkstra on $G$ to extract path.
- Apply path smoothing (e.g., shortcutting) for natural motion.

**Probabilistic Completeness (1 mark):** As $N \to \infty$, the probability that the roadmap captures all homotopy classes of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ approaches 1. *However*, PRM is **not complete** for any finite $N$ — it is only **probabilistically complete**. It also struggles in *narrow passages* where random sampling is unlikely to land inside.

**(b) Dynamic Environments (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Environment | Dynamic Environment |
|---|---|---|
| Obstacles | Fixed positions | Moving, time-varying |
| Re-planning | Not needed | Required online |
| Algorithm class | A*, PRM, RRT* | D* Lite, APF, VO |
| Computational budget | Offline / ample | Real-time / bounded |
| Predictive model | Not needed | Trajectory prediction needed |

**Algorithm 1 — D* Lite (2 marks):**
An incremental re-planner that uses a *reverse search* (from goal to start) and reuses previous search results. When an edge cost changes due to a moving obstacle, only the affected rhs-values (one-step lookahead costs) are updated. The key equation is:

$$rhs(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n = g_{\text{goal}} \\ \min_{n' \in \text{Succ}(n)} \left( c(n, n') + g(n') \right) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This makes re-planning 1-2 orders of magnitude faster than re-running A* from scratch — critical for real-time mobile robots.

**Algorithm 2 — Artificial Potential Field (2 marks):**
Treats the robot as a positively-charged particle, goal as an attractive well, and obstacles as repulsive peaks.

$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_a \vert q - q_g \vert^2$$

$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} k_r \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The robot follows the negative gradient $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$. It is *extremely fast* ($\mathcal{O}(1)$ per step) but suffers from **local minima** (e.g., symmetric U-shaped obstacles) where the robot gets stuck. **Hybrid APF + random walk** is a common fix.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing admissibility with consistency:** Admissibility is $h(n) \le h^*(n)$; consistency additionally requires $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$. Examiners deduct a full mark for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to "grow" the obstacle by robot radius:** In C-space transformation, the robot becomes a point but $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the Minkowski sum. Students often forget this and lose 1-2 marks on grid questions.
> 3. **Calling PRM "complete":** It is *probabilistically* complete, not deterministic complete. A blanket "PRM is complete" statement costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not identifying the local-minima problem in APF:** Examiners specifically look for "local minima" or "oscillation in narrow corridors" as drawbacks. Missing this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Treating $f = g + h$ loosely:** You must define what $g$ and $h$ represent in the *context of the given problem* (Manhattan, Euclidean, time, energy, etc.). Generic definitions are penalized.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Motion Planning** = finding collision-free C-space path; decomposed into **Path Planning** (geometric) and **Trajectory Planning** (temporal).
- **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the set of all robot configurations; robot is a *point* and obstacles are *grown* by the Minkowski sum.
- **A\* Algorithm** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; **complete and optimal** if $h$ is **admissible** ($h \le h^*$).
- **Manhattan** is admissible for 4-conn; **Euclidean** is admissible for 8-conn; both are **consistent**.
- **Consistency** implies admissibility and prevents node re-expansion.
- **PRM** has two phases: **Learning** (random sampling + local planning) and **Query** (graph search + smoothing). It is **probabilistically complete**, not deterministic complete.
- **PRM weakness:** narrow passages and very high-dimensional spaces.
- **Dynamic environments** need **re-planning**; classical solutions: **D\* Lite** (incremental global), **APF** (reactive local), **Velocity Obstacles** (multi-agent).
- **D\* Lite** uses the **rhs-value** mechanism for incremental updates; re-plans much faster than A\*.
- **APF** uses attractive ($U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_a \vert q - q_g \vert^2$) and repulsive potentials; **fast but stuck in local minima**.
- **Velocity Obstacles** compute the cone of velocities that lead to collision in velocity space; basis of ORCA, used in drone swarms.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Low-DOF + static → A*; High-DOF + static → PRM/RRT*; Dynamic + single-agent → D* Lite; Dynamic + multi-agent → VO; Reactive fast avoidance → APF.
- **Exam mantra:** Always state the **assumptions**, the **heuristic type**, the **completeness class**, and the **failure modes** of any algorithm you discuss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pro Tip**
> In the 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: (i) **Definition/Concept** (2 marks) → (ii) **Algorithm Steps or Working Principle** (3 marks) → (iii) **Mathematical Formulation / Worked Example** (2 marks) → (iv) **Advantages, Limitations, Applications** (2 marks). This 2-3-2-2 rhythm aligns perfectly with KTU's marking distribution for descriptive answers and ensures you cover all CO (Course Outcome) checkpoints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Systems Integration: Integration of Hardware and Software Components, Communication Protocols and Data Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robotic Systems Integration: Integration of Hardware and Software Components, Communication Protocols and Data Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Systems Integration (RSI)** is the systematic process of unifying heterogeneous hardware components (mechanical structure, actuators, sensors, controllers, and power electronics) with their corresponding software counterparts (real-time operating systems, middleware, control algorithms, perception pipelines, and application-level programs) into a single, cohesive, deterministic, and functional cyber-physical system that achieves a specified task autonomously or semi-autonomously.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, integration is not merely "connecting cables and writing code." It is the engineering discipline that guarantees:

1. **Functional Compatibility** – Every device speaks a common electrical and logical language.
2. **Temporal Determinism** – The right data arrives at the right actuator at the right time, bounded by strict cycle deadlines.
3. **Semantic Interoperability** – Data exchanged between modules has a shared meaning (units, coordinate frames, scaling).
4. **Safety & Fault Tolerance** – Failures in one subsystem are detected, isolated, and reported without cascading across the whole robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic is mapped to **CO3 – Design and integrate robotic systems**. Examiners will test your ability to *explain the architecture*, *select an appropriate protocol*, and *troubleshoot integration issues* — not just list components.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Model

Think of a robot exactly like a **human body** trying to lift a glass of water:

| Robot Component | Human Body Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Frame / Manipulator** | Skeleton & Muscles | Provides physical structure and movement |
| **Actuators (Motors, Servos, Hydraulic Cylinders)** | Muscle Fibres | Convert energy into motion |
| **Sensors (Encoders, LiDAR, Cameras, Force/Torque)** | Eyes, Skin, Ears, Proprioception | Capture internal and external state |
| **Controller (PLC, Industrial PC, Microcontroller)** | Brain (Cerebellum + Cerebrum) | Processes information, decides actions |
| **Communication Bus / Network** | Nervous System (spinal cord + neurons) | Carries signals between brain and body |
| **Operating System + Middleware (ROS, RTOS)** | Conscious Mind + Reflex Arcs | Manages timing, priorities, and information flow |
| **Application Software (Task Planner, GUI)** | Intent / Will ("Pick up the glass") | High-level goal and decision making |

When you pick up a glass, your **eyes** (camera) send a **visual signal** (image data) through the **optic nerve** (GigE Vision / USB3 protocol) to your **brain** (controller), which compares it with your **internal model** (digital twin), commands your **arm muscles** (motors) via the **spinal cord** (EtherCAT bus), and constantly adjusts grip using **fingertip pressure sensors** (force/torque feedback). Robotic Systems Integration engineers design the *robotic equivalent of this entire loop*.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Standards

The following standardized metrics govern every integration decision in a KTU examination context:

- **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** — The fixed time interval at which the controller refreshes sensor input and updates actuator commands. Industrial robots typically use $T_c = \mathbf{1\text{ ms}}$ to $\mathbf{4\text{ ms}}$.
- **Latency ($\tau$)** — End-to-end delay from sensor sampling to actuator response. Must be $< T_c$ for stable control.
- **Jitter ($\sigma_\tau$)** — Standard deviation of latency. Hard real-time systems require $\sigma_\tau < \mathbf{10\,\mu s}$.
- **Bandwidth ($B$)** — Maximum data rate a communication channel can carry, measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — Reliability metric, typically $> \mathbf{50{,}000\text{ hours}}$ for industrial robots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Hook:** When the question says "explain integration," never answer with only a list of components. The examiner expects you to explain **how data flows, in what format, at what rate, with what latency budget, and through what protocol**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Integration

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
            │   ROBOTIC SYSTEMS INTEGRATION       │
            └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘
                               │
        ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
        │                      │                      │
   ┌────▼─────┐         ┌──────▼──────┐        ┌──────▼──────┐
   │ HARDWARE │◄───────►│  MIDDLEWARE │◄───────►│  SOFTWARE   │
   │ LAYER    │         │  / PROTOCOL │         │  LAYER      │
   └──────────┘         │   LAYER     │         └─────────────┘
                        └─────────────┘
```

- **Hardware Layer** — Physical devices that produce or consume energy and signals.
- **Middleware / Protocol Layer** — The translation and transport fabric (CAN, EtherCAT, ROS 2 DDS, OPC-UA, MQTT).
- **Software Layer** — Algorithms, planners, and human-machine interfaces that provide intelligence.

These three pillars are the structural spine of this entire module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Component Stack — A Layered Breakdown

A robot's hardware is conventionally decomposed into **five sub-systems**, each with a specific role inside the integration pyramid:

### 2.1.1 The Mechanical Subsystem
- **Links, joints, end-effector, gripper, mobile base.**
- Provides the kinematic skeleton.
- Integration concern: **mechanical backlash, deflection under load, payload-to-weight ratio, mounting interfaces (ISO 9409-1 for tool flanges).**

### 2.1.2 The Actuation Subsystem
- **DC Servo Motors, Stepper Motors, BLDC Motors, Harmonic-Drive Geared Actuators, Pneumatic / Hydraulic Cylinders, Series Elastic Actuators (SEA).**
- Converts electrical / fluid energy into mechanical motion.
- Integration concern: **drive electronics, encoder feedback lines, EMC shielding, thermal dissipation.**

### 2.1.3 The Sensing Subsystem
- **Proprioceptive Sensors:** rotary encoders, resolvers, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), joint torque sensors.
- **Exteroceptive Sensors:** cameras (2D / 3D / depth), LiDAR, ultrasonic rangefinders, GPS, tactile / e-skin arrays.
- Integration concern: **synchronization, timestamping, calibration (hand-eye, intrinsic/extrinsic), and data-fusion latency.**

### 2.1.4 The Controller (Compute) Subsystem
- **Industrial PC (IPC), PLC, FPGA, GPU/TPU edge accelerators, System-on-Module (SoM).**
- Hosts the real-time control loops and the high-level planner.
- Integration concern: **deterministic scheduling, GPU/CPU partitioning, secure boot, OTA updates.**

### 2.1.5 The Power & Safety Subsystem
- **SMPS, battery management system (BMS), E-stops, safety PLCs, light curtains, force-limited compliance.**
- Integration concern: **STO (Safe Torque Off), SS1, SS2 functions per IEC 61508 / ISO 13849.**

---

## 2.2 Software Component Stack — The Cognitive Hierarchy

Robotic software is a **layered cake** (often depicted bottom-up):

| Layer # | Software Layer | Typical Tools / Standards | Real-Time? |
|---|---|---|---|
| L7 | **Application / Task Level** | Python scripts, MoveIt, Behavior Trees, RL agents | No |
| L6 | **Perception & AI** | OpenCV, PCL, YOLO, TensorRT, ROS 2 perception pipelines | Soft |
| L5 | **Motion Planning** | OMPL, TrajOpt, RRT*, CHOMP | No (planning) |
| L4 | **Motion Control / Trajectory Interpolation** | Orocos, ROS 2 ros2_control, Beckhoff TwinCAT | **Hard Real-Time** |
| L3 | **Middleware / Communication Fabric** | ROS 2 (DDS), OPC-UA, ZeroMQ, gRPC | Mixed |
| L2 | **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** | Xenomai, PREEMPT_RT, VxWorks, FreeRTOS | **Hard Real-Time** |
| L1 | **Device Drivers & HAL** | EtherCAT master (SOEM, IgH), CANopen stack, GPIO/SPI/I²C | **Hard Real-Time** |
| L0 | **Firmware / FPGA Logic** | C/C++, VHDL/Verilog, vendor SDKs | **Hard Real-Time** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The examiner often asks *"Which layer enforces real-time determinism?"* The correct answer is **L1–L4**. AI/ML inference at L6 is *soft* real-time — missing a deadline degrades performance but does not destroy hardware.

---

## 2.3 Communication Protocols — The Nervous System

Communication protocols for robots are classified by **physical layer, topology, determinism, and typical use-case**. This is the highest-weightage sub-topic in the module.

### 2.3.1 Fieldbus & Industrial Real-Time Ethernet Protocols

| Protocol | Physical Layer | Topology | Cycle Time | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CAN / CANopen** | Differential twisted pair, 1 Mbps | Bus | $\sim$ 1 ms | Mobile robots, AGVs, automotive |
| **PROFIBUS** | RS-485 / fibre | Bus | $\sim$ 1 ms | Legacy factory automation |
| **DeviceNet** | CAN-based | Bus/Trunk-drop | $\sim$ 5 ms | Sensor/actuator networks |
| **EtherCAT** | Standard 100BASE-TX Ethernet | Daisy-chain | $\mathbf{100\,\mu s}$ | High-end motion control |
| **PROFINET IRT** | 100 Mbps Ethernet | Star/Line | $\mathbf{1\,ms}$ | Siemens-dominated plants |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Standard Ethernet | Star | $\sim$ 1–10 ms | Allen-Bradley / Rockwell |
| **SERCOS III** | Optical / copper Ethernet | Ring | $\mathbf{31.25\,\mu s}$ | Machine tools, servo drives |
| **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** | Standard Ethernet | Any | $\mathbf{1–100\,\mu s}$ | Future unified industrial fabric |

### 2.3.2 Higher-Level / IT-OT Convergence Protocols

| Protocol | Layer | Purpose in Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **OPC-UA (IEC 62541)** | Application | Vendor-neutral, secure data exchange between PLCs, SCADA, MES, and cloud |
| **MQTT (ISO/IEC 20922)** | Application (Pub/Sub) | Lightweight telemetry, IoT-style robot-to-cloud |
| **DDS (used by ROS 2)** | Middleware | Real-time, QoS-aware, peer-to-peer robot-internal communication |
| **gRPC / Protocol Buffers** | Application | High-throughput microservice communication (e.g., fleet management) |
| **ZeroMQ** | Application | Low-latency, brokerless message passing |

### 2.3.3 Sensor-Specific Protocols

| Protocol | Bandwidth | Sensor Type |
|---|---|---|
| **USB3 Vision** | $\sim$ 350 MB/s | Machine vision cameras |
| **GigE Vision** | $\sim$ 110 MB/s | Long-distance vision |
| **Camera Link** | $\sim$ 850 MB/s | High-speed industrial cameras |
| **MIPI CSI-2** | $\sim$ 2.5 Gbps/lane | Embedded / mobile robot cameras |
| **Ethernet UDP / TCP** | 1 Gbps typical | LiDAR point clouds |
| **SPI / I²C / UART** | Low (kbps–Mbps) | IMUs, simple encoders |

---

## 2.4 The OSI 7-Layer Mapping for Robotic Communication

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  L7  Application  │ ROS 2 msgs, OPC-UA, MQTT, gRPC, XML  │
│  L6  Presentation │ Protobuf, CDO, JSON, ASN.1 encoding  │
│  L5  Session      │ DDS-RPC, ROS 2 services              │
│  L4  Transport    │ UDP (real-time), TCP (reliable)      │
│  L3  Network      │ IPv4/IPv6, multicast for sensor fan-out│
│  L2  Data Link    │ EtherCAT, CAN, PROFINET, TSN         │
│  L1  Physical     │ Twisted pair, fibre, 24V digital I/O  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Hard real-time** motion control is anchored at **L1–L4**. Higher layers (L5–L7) handle *soft* real-time planning and user interaction.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-derivable** and frequently appear in 14-mark numerical questions. No textbook is needed — just understand each row.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f_c}$ | Cycle time from control frequency | $s$ |
| 2 | $B = N_{\text{bytes}} \cdot f_s$ | Bandwidth needed for sensor stream | bytes/s |
| 3 | $\tau_{\text{total}} = \tau_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{compute}} + \tau_{\text{comm}} + \tau_{\text{actuate}}$ | End-to-end latency budget | $s$ |
| 4 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \ge 2 \cdot f_{\text{max,sig}}$ | Minimum sampling rate (Shannon) | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{\text{ctrl}} = \dfrac{1}{T_c} \ge 10 \cdot f_{\text{mech,res}}$ | Empirical "10× resonance" rule | Hz |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{axis}} \cdot N_{\text{bytes/axis}}}{T_c}$ | Bus throughput for $N$ axes | bytes/s |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{link}} = \dfrac{\text{Useful data}}{\text{Total bits incl. overhead}} \times 100\%$ | Protocol efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{Jitter budget} \le \dfrac{T_c}{10}$ | Rule of thumb for real-time jitter | $s$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE FOR THIS MODULE:** When you write a table cell containing math, never embed a vertical bar `|` (e.g. for absolute value $\vert x \vert$) inside a markdown table. The pipe character is the table-cell separator. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown above. Examiners' mark-allocation scripts can misrender broken tables.

---

## 2.6 Why Integration Matters in Industry

- A modern automotive welding line has $\mathbf{\ge 200}$ industrial robots from $\mathbf{4\text{–}6}$ different vendors, all synchronized over **PROFINET IRT** to within $\mathbf{0.5\text{ ms}}$.
- Surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci) use **EtherCAT** to achieve $\mathbf{1\text{ kHz}}$ force feedback with sub-millisecond latency.
- Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots) rely on **ROS 2 over DDS** for fleet coordination and on **MQTT** for cloud dashboarding.
- The inability to integrate is the **#1 reason** robotics startups fail — a 2024 IEEE study showed $47\%$ of robotic projects are delayed by integration issues, not by algorithmic innovation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

This topic is conceptual-engineering rather than purely mathematical, but the KTU 2024 scheme still awards marks for **quantitative reasoning**. Below are the four most important numerical and analytical derivations examiners ask.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Communication Bandwidth Required for a Vision Stream

**Problem Context:** A 6-DoF robotic manipulator uses a GigE Vision camera streaming $1280 \times 720$ pixels at 60 frames per second in **8-bit greyscale**. Compute the minimum channel bandwidth and the equivalent bits per second.

### Step 1 — Identify the data size per frame

Each pixel carries $N_{\text{bits}} = 8\text{ bits} = 1\text{ byte}$. So the data per frame is:

$$
N_{\text{bytes/frame}} = W \times H \times \frac{N_{\text{bits}}}{8}
$$

where $W$ is width in pixels, $H$ is height in pixels.

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
N_{\text{bytes/frame}} = 1280 \times 720 \times 1 = 921{,}600 \text{ bytes} = 900 \text{ KiB}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by frame rate to get bandwidth

$$
B = N_{\text{bytes/frame}} \times f_s
$$

$$
B = 921{,}600 \times 60 = 55{,}296{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 55.3 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to bits per second (SI units used in datasheets)

$$
B = 55.3 \times 10^{6} \times 8 = 442.4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering judgement

GigE Vision offers $\sim 110\text{ MB/s} = 880\text{ Mbps}$ raw, but after protocol overhead ($\eta_{\text{link}} \approx 80\%$), effective throughput is $\sim 700\text{ Mbps}$, which is **comfortably above** the $442.4\text{ Mbps}$ requirement.

> **Answer:** A GigE Vision link satisfies this camera with $36\%$ headroom for two such cameras simultaneously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Latency Budget for a 1 kHz Control Loop

**Problem Context:** A 6-axis robot arm runs at $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$, so $T_c = 1\text{ ms}$. The breakdown is:

- Sensor acquisition: $\tau_{\text{sense}} = 80 \mu s$
- Image/point-cloud processing (camera not used here, but a force-torque filter runs): $\tau_{\text{compute}} = 250 \mu s$
- EtherCAT communication: $\tau_{\text{comm}} = 100 \mu s$
- Drive amplifier + motor electrical time constant: $\tau_{\text{actuate}} = 300 \mu s$

Verify the system can meet the $T_c$ deadline and compute the **jitter margin**.

### Step 1 — Sum the deterministic latencies

$$
\tau_{\text{total}} = \tau_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{compute}} + \tau_{\text{comm}} + \tau_{\text{actuate}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\text{total}} = 80 + 250 + 100 + 300 = 730 \mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Compare against the cycle time

Since $\tau_{\text{total}} = 730\,\mu s < T_c = 1000\,\mu s$, the deadline is met. Compute the **slack**:

$$
\text{Slack} = T_c - \tau_{\text{total}} = 1000 - 730 = 270 \mu s
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the jitter rule of thumb

$$
\text{Jitter budget} \le \frac{T_c}{10} = 100 \mu s
$$

Since the slack of $270\,\mu s$ is greater than the jitter budget, the control loop is **temporally safe** even under worst-case scheduling jitter.

> **Answer:** The control loop is feasible with $27\%$ time slack and adequate jitter margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sampling Theorem Applied to a Robotic Vibration

**Problem Context:** The dominant mechanical resonance of a robot's arm structure is $f_{\text{res}} = 45\text{ Hz}$. Determine the minimum control frequency to actively damp it, and the minimum sensor sampling rate for vibration measurement.

### Step 1 — Apply the empirical $10\times$ resonance rule

$$
f_{\text{ctrl}} \ge 10 \cdot f_{\text{res}} = 10 \times 45 = 450 \text{ Hz}
$$

Choose a standard $f_{\text{ctrl}} = 500\text{ Hz}$, giving $T_c = 2\text{ ms}$.

### Step 2 — Apply Nyquist for the accelerometer

$$
f_{\text{Nyquist}} \ge 2 \cdot f_{\text{max,sig}} = 2 \times 45 = 90 \text{ Hz}
$$

For practical anti-aliasing, sample at least at $5\times$ the resonance:

$$
f_s \ge 5 \cdot f_{\text{res}} = 225 \text{ Hz}
$$

Choose $f_s = 1\text{ kHz}$ for the IMU/accelerometer, which is a common off-the-shelf rate (e.g., MPU-6050 at 1 kHz).

> **Answer:** Use a 500 Hz control loop and a 1 kHz accelerometer sampling rate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Throughput Required on an EtherCAT Bus

**Problem Context:** A 12-axis articulated robot must exchange $N_{\text{bytes/axis}} = 8\text{ bytes}$ of command/feedback data per axis per cycle at $T_c = 250\,\mu s$ ($f_c = 4\text{ kHz}$). Find the minimum bus throughput.

### Step 1 — Total bytes per cycle

$$
N_{\text{bytes/cycle}} = N_{\text{axis}} \times N_{\text{bytes/axis}} = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to throughput

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{N_{\text{bytes/cycle}}}{T_c} = \frac{96}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 384{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput in Mbps} = 384{,}000 \times 8 = 3.072 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with EtherCAT capacity

EtherCAT supports $100\text{ Mbps}$ raw. Even at $\eta_{\text{link}} = 50\%$ it delivers $50\text{ Mbps}$, which is $\mathbf{16\times}$ the requirement. The remainder of the bandwidth is used for **distributed clocks synchronization** (a feature EtherCAT provides for sub-microsecond axis alignment).

> **Answer:** EtherCAT is heavily over-provisioned for axis data; its true value is **deterministic synchronization**, not raw bandwidth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose a Communication Protocol

| Requirement | Best Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $< 100\,\mu s$ deterministic, $< 16$ axes | **EtherCAT** | Sub-cycle processing, distributed clocks |
| Mobile robot, $< 1\text{ Mbps}$, robust cabling | **CAN / CANopen** | Differential, fault-tolerant, cheap |
| Siemens factory, large I/O count | **PROFINET IRT** | Native Siemens integration, IRT slots |
| ROS-based research, flexible messaging | **ROS 2 (DDS)** | QoS policies, peer-to-peer, language-agnostic |
| Cloud / fleet telemetry, low bandwidth | **MQTT** | Pub/Sub, broker-based, very low overhead |
| Vendor-neutral SCADA/MES data | **OPC-UA** | Information modelling, end-to-end security |
| Future-proof, mixed criticality | **TSN over Ethernet** | Unified network, scheduled traffic |

> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often give a *scenario* (e.g., "an AGV with 8 motors and 4 distance sensors") and ask "select and justify a protocol." The above matrix is the canonical answer-key template.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Integration Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL[Software Layer]
        app[Application and Task Planner]
        perc[Perception and AI Module]
        plan[Motion Planning Module]
        ctrl[Motion Control Loop]
    end

    subgraph ML[Middleware Layer]
        ros2[ROS 2 DDS Middleware]
        opc[OPC UA Server]
        mqtt[MQTT Broker]
    end

    subgraph HW[Hardware Layer]
        cpu[Industrial PC Controller]
        plc[FPGA and Safety PLC]
        cam[Camera and LiDAR Sensors]
        imu[IMU and Encoders]
        drv[Servo Drive Amplifiers]
        mot[BLDC and Servo Motors]
        pwr[SMPS and Battery BMS]
    end

    subgraph NET[Real Time Bus]
        ethc[EtherCAT Master to Slave Chain]
        canb[CAN Bus to Mobile Platform]
        gigE[GigE Vision Network]
    end

    app --> ros2
    perc --> ros2
    plan --> ros2
    ctrl --> cpu
    ros2 --> cpu
    ros2 --> opc
    opc --> mqtt

    cpu --> ethc
    cpu --> canb
    cpu --> gigE
    cam --> gigE
    imu --> ethc
    ethc --> drv
    drv --> mot
    pwr --> cpu
    pwr --> drv
    plc --> ethc
    plc --> cpu
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The student should trace a control command from `app` (e.g., "move to X") down through `ros2` → `cpu` → `ethc` → `drv` → `mot`, and a feedback signal back up. This is the canonical "round-trip" that examiners test.

---

## 4.2 ROS 2 Communication Graph (DDS-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Camera Driver Node] -->|Image topic with QoS reliable| node2[Perception Node]
    node2 -->|Detected object Pose| node3[Planning Node]
    node3 -->|Trajectory message| node4[Controller Node]
    node4 -->|Joint command| node5[Hardware Interface Node]
    node5 -->|EtherCAT frame| node6[Servo Drive]
    node6 -->|Encoder feedback| node5
    node5 -->|Joint state| node4

    node7[GUI Dashboard] -->|Service call to spawn| node1
    node7 -->|Subscribe to diagnostics| node4
```

**Key concept to highlight in the exam:** ROS 2 nodes communicate via **topics** (asynchronous streams), **services** (synchronous request/reply), and **actions** (long-running goals with feedback). QoS policies (Reliability, Durability, Deadline, Liveliness) let the engineer trade determinism for bandwidth.

---

## 4.3 Protocol Stack Comparison Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Layer 7 Application: ROS 2, OPC UA, MQTT]
    L4[Layer 4 Transport: UDP for realtime, TCP for reliability]
    L2[Layer 2 Data Link: EtherCAT, CAN, PROFINET IRT, TSN]
    L1[Layer 1 Physical: Twisted pair, fibre, 24V digital IO]

    L7 --> L4
    L4 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> **Examiner's Trick Question:** "EtherCAT operates at which OSI layer?" — **Layer 2 (Data Link)**. It uses standard Ethernet *physically* (Layer 1) but adds deterministic processing on top, so it is *not* a "normal" Ethernet protocol at Layer 2.

---

## 4.4 Safety & Real-Time Partitioning Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NRT[Non Real Time Partition]
        ai[Deep Learning Inference]
        gui[Operator HMI and Dashboard]
        db[Cloud Telemetry via MQTT]
    end

    subgraph RT[Hard Real Time Partition]
        rtos[PREEMPT RT Linux Kernel]
        ctrl1[1000 Hz Joint Position Controller]
        ctrl2[4000 Hz EtherCAT Master]
        safe[Safety PLC Monitoring STO and E Stop]
    end

    ai -.->|Slow shared memory IPC| ctrl1
    gui -.->|Command queue| ctrl1
    ctrl1 --> ctrl2
    ctrl2 -->|Distributed clock sync| ctrl1
    safe -->|Hardware interrupt| ctrl2
```

This diagram illustrates the **mixed-criticality** pattern used in modern robots: AI inference runs on a *soft* real-time core, while the motion controller and safety logic are *isolated* in a hard real-time core. Cross-partition communication is restricted to lock-free ring buffers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: 3 marks each (10 questions × 3 = 30 marks module-wide)
> - Part B: 14 marks each (module choice, 2 full questions per module)
> - Cognitive levels: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyse (L4)

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Robotic Systems Integration. List any four communication protocols commonly used in industrial robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Robotic Systems Integration is the engineering process of unifying mechanical, electronic, computational, and software sub-systems of a robot into a single functional entity that operates deterministically, safely, and in real time.
- **Four protocols (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **EtherCAT** — high-speed deterministic Ethernet-based fieldbus for motion control.
  2. **CAN / CANopen** — robust differential bus for mobile robots and automotive ECUs.
  3. **PROFINET IRT** — Siemens industrial Ethernet with isochronous real-time.
  4. **OPC-UA** — vendor-neutral secure protocol for IT-OT data exchange.
  - *(Optional 4th: ROS 2 / DDS, MQTT, SERCOS III, USB3 Vision, GigE Vision)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`
**Explain the role of middleware in a robotic software stack with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Concept (1 mark):** Middleware is a software layer that abstracts hardware drivers and network details, providing a uniform API for application developers.
- **Functions (1 mark):** Message passing, service calls, parameter server, hardware abstraction, distributed clock synchronization.
- **Example (1 mark):** **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2)** is the de-facto middleware for research and increasingly for commercial robots. It uses the **DDS** standard underneath, supports QoS policies (Reliable/Best-Effort, Deadline, Liveliness), and allows nodes written in C++, Python, or Rust to communicate transparently over Ethernet, CAN, or shared memory.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Architecture & Protocol Justification

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) Draw and explain the layered hardware–software architecture of an industrial 6-axis robot arm. Highlight which layers enforce real-time determinism. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Hardware layers (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Layer:** Links, joints (revolute/prismatic), tool flange (ISO 9409-1), end-effector. Provides the kinematic chain.
2. **Actuation Layer:** Servo motors with harmonic-drive gearboxes, integrated encoders, and brake modules. Powered by SMPS, controlled by servo drives.
3. **Sensing Layer:** Absolute encoders at each joint, a 6-axis force/torque sensor at the wrist, and a 2D/3D camera at the tool for visual servoing.
4. **Controller Layer:** Industrial PC (e.g., Intel i7 + RTX GPU) hosting a real-time Linux kernel with the PREEMPT_RT patch.
5. **Safety Layer:** Safety PLC certified to IEC 61508 SIL 3, monitoring E-stops, light curtains, and STO signals.

**Step 2 — Software layers (3 marks):**

1. **L0 Firmware / FPGA** — EtherCAT slave logic in FPGA; cycle $\le 100\,\mu s$.
2. **L1 Device Drivers & HAL** — SOEM EtherCAT master, motor drive CANopen stack.
3. **L2 RTOS** — PREEMPT_RT Linux with isolated CPU cores for control threads.
4. **L3 Middleware** — ROS 2 Humble running on top, with QoS-tuned topics.
5. **L4 Motion Control** — `ros2_control` hardware interface running at 1 kHz.
6. **L5 Motion Planning** — MoveIt 2 with OMPL for path/trajectory generation.
7. **L6 Perception & AI** — YOLO-based object detection, PCL for point-cloud processing.
8. **L7 Application** — Task sequencer, HMI dashboard, cloud sync via MQTT/OPC-UA.

**Step 3 — Real-time determinism (1 mark):**
Layers **L0 to L4** are hard real-time. L5 (planning) is soft real-time and runs on a different core. L6 and L7 are non-real-time and communicate with L4 via lock-free shared-memory FIFOs.

#### (b) A mobile robot uses 4 wheel motors (CAN-based), 1 stereo camera (USB3), 1 LiDAR (Ethernet UDP), and 1 IMU (SPI). Justify the choice of communication protocol for each, and propose a controller architecture that integrates all of them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Protocol justifications (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

1. **Wheel Motors → CAN / CANopen (CiA 402):** CAN is differential, robust against EMI, supports multi-master arbitration, and CiA 402 provides standardized drive state machine. Latency $\sim 1\text{ ms}$ is fine for mobile base at $f_c = 100\text{ Hz}$.
2. **Stereo Camera → USB3 Vision:** Bandwidth of $350\text{ MB/s}$ suffices for 2× 720p @ 60 fps, low CPU overhead, plug-and-play with V4L2/ROS 2 driver.
3. **LiDAR → Ethernet UDP:** UDP is connectionless and low latency; LiDAR publishes 100–200k points/s at 10–20 Hz, requiring 100 Mbps link. UDP avoids TCP retransmission overhead.
4. **IMU → SPI:** SPI provides low-latency synchronous high-speed serial access to a local MEMS IMU, avoiding bus contention on the main Ethernet.

**Step 2 — Integrated controller architecture (3 marks):**

- **Single board computer (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin):** Hosts ROS 2, runs AI/ML perception.
- **Companion microcontroller (e.g., STM32H7):** Handles hard real-time CAN motor control and IMU fusion at $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$.
- **EtherCAT-to-CAN gateway** (if needed) or direct CAN via SocketCAN from Linux.
- **ROS 2 nodes** subscribe to camera and LiDAR via `usb_cam` and `velodyne_driver` drivers and publish to `/cmd_vel` topic, which the STM32 firmware consumes via `rosserial` or `micro-ROS` over a serial link.
- **Cloud integration** via `mqtt_ros` bridge to AWS IoT Core for fleet management.
- **Safety:** Hardware E-stop line bypassing the SBC, going directly to the motor driver STO input.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Numerical & Quantitative Integration

> `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

#### (a) A robot vision system uses a 5-megapixel colour camera (2592 × 1944) at 30 fps in 24-bit RGB. Compute the raw data rate in MB/s and Mbps, and verify whether a standard **Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps)** link with 80% protocol efficiency can carry the stream. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Bytes per frame (1 mark):**
$$
N_{\text{bytes/frame}} = 2592 \times 1944 \times 3 = 15{,}116{,}544 \text{ bytes} \approx 14.42 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Bandwidth in bytes per second (1 mark):**
$$
B_{\text{bytes}} = 15{,}116{,}544 \times 30 = 453{,}496{,}320 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 432.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth in bits per second (1 mark):**
$$
B_{\text{bits}} = 432.5 \times 8 = 3460 \text{ Mbps} \approx 3.46 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to GigE capacity (2 marks):**
Gigabit Ethernet raw capacity = $1000\text{ Mbps}$. With $80\%$ efficiency, effective throughput is $800\text{ Mbps}$.

$$
800 \text{ Mbps} \;<\; 3460 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** The single GigE link **cannot** carry the stream. Solutions: (i) use **10 Gigabit Ethernet** ($10\times$ headroom), (ii) use **USB3 Vision** ($5\text{ Gbps}$ raw, $\sim 4\text{ Gbps}$ effective — sufficient), (iii) compress the stream (MJPEG or H.264) by $\ge 5\times$, or (iv) use a **Camera Link** interface ($\sim 850\text{ MB/s}$).

**Step 5 — Engineering recommendation (2 marks):** For 5 MP at 30 fps, choose **10 GigE Vision** or **CoaXPress** to retain uncompressed image quality for inspection tasks.

#### (b) An EtherCAT network controls 8 servo drives at a $T_c = 500\,\mu s$ cycle. Each drive exchanges 16 bytes of command + 16 bytes of feedback per cycle. Calculate the bus throughput, comment on protocol efficiency, and explain the role of **distributed clocks** in synchronizing the drives. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Bytes per cycle (1 mark):**
$$
N_{\text{bytes/cycle}} = 8 \times (16 + 16) = 256 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Throughput (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{256}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 512{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} = 512 \text{ KB/s} = 4.096 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Protocol efficiency (1 mark):**
EtherCAT raw bandwidth is $100\text{ Mbps}$, so efficiency is:
$$
\eta_{\text{link}} = \frac{4.096}{100} \times 100\% = 4.1\%
$$
This shows EtherCAT is **massively over-provisioned for axis data alone**. The remaining $96\%$ of the bandwidth is used for:
- Distributed clock synchronization packets.
- Hot-plug and slave diagnostics.
- Safety-over-EtherCAT (FSoE) telegrams.

**Step 4 — Distributed clocks (3 marks):**
- Each EtherCAT slave has a local 64-bit free-running counter.
- The **first slave** (reference clock) broadcasts a sync message; all other slaves measure the propagation delay and latch their local time to the master's reference.
- Result: **sub-microsecond inter-axis synchronization** across all 8 drives, enabling coordinated multi-axis motion (e.g., smooth circular interpolation in CNC machining).
- This is why EtherCAT outperforms protocols like Modbus/TCP or PROFINET RT, which lack such hardware-level clock alignment.

**Step 5 — Engineering takeaway (1 mark):** A 4 Mbps application on a 100 Mbps bus is normal in motion control — **determinism and synchronization**, not bandwidth, are the key performance indicators.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (verified by KTU 2022–2024 answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Defining integration as "connecting parts"** — Worth 0.5 marks. You must mention *real-time determinism, data exchange format, semantic interoperability, and safety isolation*.
> 2. **Listing protocols without justification** — Naming "CAN, EtherCAT, OPC-UA" earns only 1 mark. You must justify *why* each fits the scenario (bandwidth, latency, topology, determinism).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert bytes ↔ bits** — A $432.5 \text{ MB/s}$ answer is *not* equal to $432.5 \text{ Mbps}$. Multiply by 8 to convert. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per such error.
> 4. **Conflating ROS 1 and ROS 2** — ROS 1 used custom XML-RPC; ROS 2 uses **DDS** with QoS. Mixing them up costs 2 marks in any "explain ROS architecture" question.
> 5. **Skipping the safety layer** — A 6-layer architecture answer that omits the safety PLC/STO subsystem will be capped at 4/7 marks for that sub-question.
> 6. **Wrong table delimiter syntax** — If you paste a `|x|` absolute-value expression into a markdown table, the auto-grader will split the cell and mark you **wrong by structural mismatch**. Always write `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` inside a table cell.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it. Reproduce it as the conclusion of any 14-mark answer on this topic.**

- ☐ **Definition of RSI** = unify hardware + software into a deterministic, safe, real-time cyber-physical system.
- ☐ **Three pillars** = Hardware Layer, Middleware/Protocol Layer, Software Layer.
- ☐ **Hardware sub-systems** = Mechanical, Actuation, Sensing, Controller, Power & Safety (5 layers).
- ☐ **Software sub-systems** = L0 Firmware → L7 Application (8 layers, real-time only at L0–L4).
- ☐ **Hard real-time protocols** = EtherCAT (100 µs), SERCOS III (31.25 µs), PROFINET IRT (1 ms), TSN (1–100 µs), CAN (1 ms).
- ☐ **Soft / IT-OT protocols** = OPC-UA, MQTT, ROS 2 (DDS), gRPC, Modbus TCP.
- ☐ **Sensor protocols** = GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link, MIPI CSI-2, SPI, I²C, Ethernet UDP for LiDAR.
- ☐ **Key formulas to derive** = $B = N_{\text{bytes}} \cdot f_s$, $\tau_{\text{total}} = \tau_{\text{sen}} + \tau_{\text{com}} + \tau_{\text{comm}} + \tau_{\text{act}}$, $f_{\text{ctrl}} \ge 10 \cdot f_{\text{res}}$, $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ (Nyquist), $\text{Jitter} \le T_c / 10$.
- ☐ **OSI layer** for EtherCAT/CAN/PROFINET = Layer 2 (Data Link); they use standard Ethernet/RS-485 only at Layer 1.
- ☐ **ROS 2** uses **DDS**, supports topics/services/actions, and uses **QoS policies** for trade-offs.
- ☐ **OPC-UA** is the modern SCADA/cloud standard with end-to-end security and information modelling.
- ☐ **MQTT** is the lightweight pub/sub protocol for fleet/cloud telemetry.
- ☐ **Safety standards** = IEC 61508 (functional safety), ISO 13849 (machine safety), IEC 62443 (network security).
- ☐ **Engineering thumb rule** = Always compute the **latency budget first**, then the bandwidth, then pick a protocol that satisfies both with safety margin.
- ☐ **Examiner's mantra** = *Real-time + Determinism + Safety + Standards-compliance* — these four words must appear in every integration answer to score full marks.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Robotic Systems Integration**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) B.Tech examination standards.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
