<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Robotics — Formal KTU Definition

**Industrial Robotics** is the branch of applied engineering that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of **programmable, computer-controlled manipulator systems** (robots) used to automate industrial tasks traditionally performed by human workers. According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** and the **ISO 8373:2021** standard, an industrial robot is defined as:

> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT722)** context, robotics in industry extends beyond factory floors to include **healthcare robotics, service robotics, agricultural robotics, defense robotics, and space robotics**, all unified by the core principles of **sensing, planning, and actuation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students are expected to understand *where* and *why* robots are deployed across different sectors, the *types* of robots used, *case studies* of real-world deployments, and *emerging trends* such as collaborative robots (cobots), AI-driven manipulation, and human–robot interaction (HRI).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tireless Apprentice" Mental Model

Imagine a **highly skilled craftsman apprentice** in a workshop:
- The apprentice **learns** a task by watching the master perform it once (this is like **robot programming** by demonstration or **lead-through teaching**).
- Once trained, the apprentice **repeats** the exact same task **thousands of times** without fatigue, boredom, or loss of precision (this is **repeatability** of an industrial robot).
- The apprentice uses **sensors** (eyes, ears, touch) to adapt to small changes in the workpiece (this is **closed-loop feedback control** using vision, force, and tactile sensors).
- Unlike a human, the apprentice can work in **hazardous environments** — extreme heat, toxic fumes, deep underwater, or outer space (this represents **hazardous-environment robotics**).

An industrial robot is essentially this **"tireless, sensory-equipped apprentice"**, but made of steel, motors, and microcontrollers instead of flesh and bone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The defining traits that separate a *robot* from a *machine tool* are **reprogrammability** and **versatility**. A CNC milling machine is reprogrammable but not versatile; a robotic arm is both. This distinction is **frequently asked** in KTU 2-mark and short-answer questions.

---

## 1.3 Why Industrial Robotics Matters — Engineering Motivation

| Motivation | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Productivity** | Robots operate **24 × 7** with cycle times often under **1 second** per operation. |
| **Precision** | Repeatability of modern robots is typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**, surpassing manual accuracy. |
| **Safety** | Robots perform tasks in **high-risk environments** (nuclear, mining, bomb disposal). |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | ROI (Return on Investment) for industrial robots is typically **12–24 months** in high-volume manufacturing. |
| **Quality Consistency** | Eliminates **human-induced variability** in tasks like welding, painting, and assembly. |
| **Scalability** | Robots can be **re-tasked** via software updates without physical retooling. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** (commonly tested in KTU):
> - **Payload (kg):** Maximum mass a robot can carry at full reach.
> - **Reach (mm):** Maximum distance from the robot base to the end-effector.
> - **Repeatability (mm):** The deviation in position when the robot returns to a taught point.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joints (typically **4 to 7** for industrial arms).
> - **Workspace (m³):** 3-D volume the end-effector can reach.
> - **Cycle Time (s):** Time to complete one standard pick-and-place cycle.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Industrial Robots (KTU High-Yield)

Industrial robots are classified by **kinematic structure, application domain, and control architecture**. The most important classification for KTU is by **mechanical configuration**.

### 2.1.1 By Mechanical Configuration

1. **Articulated (Jointed-Arm) Robots**
   - Structure: Series of **revolute joints** connected by rigid links (resembles a human arm).
   - Typical DOF: **6 (base, shoulder, elbow, wrist ×3)**.
   - Example: **ABB IRB 6700**, **KUKA KR 1000 Titan**.
   - Applications: Welding, painting, material handling, assembly.

2. **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)**
   - Structure: Two **parallel revolute joints** in the horizontal plane + one **prismatic joint** for vertical motion.
   - Typical DOF: **4**.
   - Applications: **Pick-and-place**, PCB assembly, small-parts insertion.
   - Advantage: Excellent **lateral rigidity** with **vertical compliance**.

3. **Cartesian (Gantry) Robots**
   - Structure: Three **prismatic joints** aligned with X, Y, Z axes.
   - Typical DOF: **3** (extendable to 4–5 with wrist).
   - Applications: CNC loading, palletizing, 3-D printing, pick-and-place in large work envelopes.

4. **Cylindrical Robots**
   - Structure: One **revolute** (base rotation) + one **prismatic** (vertical) + one **prismatic** (radial).
   - Applications: Machine tending, foundry operations, die-casting.

5. **Polar (Spherical) Robots**
   - Structure: One revolute + one prismatic + one rotational joint.
   - Applications: Forging, welding, material transfer.

6. **Delta (Parallel) Robots**
   - Structure: Three **parallel arms** connected to a common moving platform.
   - Typical DOF: **3 to 4**.
   - Applications: **High-speed packaging**, food industry, pharmaceutical sorting.
   - Speed: Can achieve **pick rates of 300+ cycles per minute**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam Trick):** Use the mnemonic **"A-S-C-C-C-D"** for the six configurations: **A**rticulated, **S**CARA, **C**artesian, **C**ylindrical, **C**/Polar, **D**elta. This is the most frequently asked classification in KTU exams.

---

### 2.1.2 By Application Domain

| Domain | Sub-Category | Typical Robot Type | Key Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Welding (arc, spot) | Articulated 6-DOF | Automotive body assembly |
| **Manufacturing** | Painting | Articulated (explosion-proof) | Automotive paint shops |
| **Manufacturing** | Material handling | Articulated, Cartesian | Palletizing boxes |
| **Healthcare** | Surgical assistance | Precision parallel / SCARA | Da Vinci Surgical System |
| **Healthcare** | Rehabilitation | Exoskeletons | ReWalk, Ekso GT |
| **Healthcare** | Pharmacy automation | Cartesian gantry | Pill dispensing systems |
| **Service** | Cleaning | Mobile manipulator | iRobot Roomba, autonomous scrubbers |
| **Agriculture** | Harvesting | Mobile manipulator | Strawberry-picking robots |
| **Defense** | EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal) | Tracked manipulator | TALON robot |
| **Space** | Planetary exploration | Rover with manipulator | NASA Perseverance, Canadarm2 |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **mathematical backbone** for KTU numerical problems in this module. Although this topic is largely descriptive, **kinematic reach, workspace volume, and cycle-time calculations** are regularly tested.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spherical Workspace Volume (Articulated Robot, full reach)** | $V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ | $R$ = maximum reach (m), Volume in m³ |
| 2 | **Cylindrical Workspace Volume** | $V_{cyl} = \pi R^{2} H$ | $R$ = radial reach, $H$ = vertical stroke |
| 3 | **Rectangular (Cartesian) Workspace Volume** | $V_{box} = L \times W \times H$ | $L, W, H$ = strokes in X, Y, Z |
| 4 | **Delta Robot Workspace (cylindrical approximation)** | $V_{delta} \approx \pi R_{max}^{2} \cdot h$ | $R_{max}$ = max radial reach, $h$ = vertical travel |
| 5 | **Repeatability vs Accuracy** | $R = 3\sigma_{pos}$ | Statistical definition (3-sigma) |
| 6 | **Cycle Time (Pick-and-Place)** | $T_{c} = T_{move} + T_{settle} + T_{return}$ | $T_{settle}$ accounts for vibration damping |
| 7 | **Payload at Reduced Reach** | $P_{eff} = P_{rated} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{R}\right)$ | $d$ = current reach, $R$ = max reach |
| 8 | **Degrees of Freedom (Grübler–Kutzbach)** | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$ | $n$ = links, $j_{1}$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_{2}$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 9 | **Joint Resolution** | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{encoder}}$ | $N_{encoder}$ = encoder pulses per revolution |
| 10 | **Surgical Robot Tremor Filtering Frequency** | $f_{cutoff} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $RC$ = filter time constant in the Da Vinci system |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **repeatability** with **accuracy**. Repeatability is the robot's ability to return to a *previously taught* point; accuracy is the ability to move to a *computed* point. Industrial robots typically have **high repeatability but moderate accuracy**.

---

## 2.3 Application Engineering Logic — The "5W+H" Framework

A well-designed robotic application must answer the **5W + H** questions. This is a KTU-favored analytical framework:

1. **WHAT** task is to be automated? (welding, painting, surgery, etc.)
2. **WHY** automate? (cost, safety, quality, throughput)
3. **WHERE** is the deployment? (factory floor, operating room, mine shaft)
4. **WHEN** does the robot operate? (continuous, shift-based, on-demand)
5. **WHO** interacts with the robot? (operator, surgeon, end-user, no one)
6. **HOW** is the task performed? (sensors, end-effectors, control algorithms)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The 5W+H framework is used in **robotic cell design** to ensure that the correct robot type, payload, reach, and end-effector are selected. A mismatch in any one parameter (e.g., choosing a 5 kg payload robot for a 10 kg part) can cause **catastrophic failure** — this is a real engineering lesson from multiple factory incidents globally.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Workspace Volume for a Spherical-Reach Articulated Robot

**Problem Statement:** An articulated 6-DOF industrial robot has a maximum reach of **$R = 1.5$ m** from the base to the wrist center. Assuming the wrist can orient freely within the full spherical envelope, compute the theoretical maximum workspace volume.

### Step 1: Identify the Geometry
A 6-DOF articulated robot with full wrist dexterity can position its end-effector anywhere on the surface of a sphere whose radius equals the maximum reach. The full reachable volume is a **solid sphere** of radius $R$.

### Step 2: Recall the Volume of a Sphere
The geometric volume of a sphere with radius $R$ is given by:

$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Value
Substituting $R = 1.5 \, \text{m}$:

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.5)^{3}$$

### Step 4: Expand the Cube
$$(1.5)^{3} = 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 = 3.375$$

### Step 5: Multiply
$$V = \frac{4}{3} \times \pi \times 3.375$$

$$V = 4 \times 3.375 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = 13.5 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = 4.5\pi$$

### Step 6: Final Numerical Result

$$V = 4.5 \pi \approx 14.137 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

> **Final Answer:** $V \approx 14.14 \, \text{m}^{3}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cylindrical Workspace for a Cylindrical-Coordinate Robot

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical-coordinate robot has a vertical stroke of **$H = 2.0$ m** and a maximum radial reach of **$R = 1.2$ m**. The base rotates $360°$. Calculate the workspace volume.

### Step 1: Geometric Identification
The workspace is a **cylinder** of radius $R$ and height $H$ (rotated about the vertical axis).

### Step 2: Cylinder Volume Formula

$$V = \pi R^{2} H$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$V = \pi (1.2)^{2} (2.0) = \pi \times 1.44 \times 2.0$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$V = 2.88\pi \approx 9.048 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

> **Final Answer:** $V \approx 9.05 \, \text{m}^{3}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Delta Robot Workspace — High-Speed Picking

**Problem Statement:** A delta robot used in pharmaceutical packaging has a maximum radial reach of **$R_{max} = 0.5$ m** and a vertical working stroke of **$h = 0.3$ m**. Approximate the workspace as a cylinder. If the robot completes **300 pick-and-place cycles per minute**, calculate the **theoretical maximum throughput in picks/hour**, assuming 100% efficiency.

### Step 1: Workspace Volume (for context only)

$$V = \pi R_{max}^{2} \cdot h = \pi (0.5)^{2} (0.3) = \pi \times 0.25 \times 0.3 = 0.075\pi$$

$$V \approx 0.2356 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

### Step 2: Throughput Calculation
Given 300 cycles per minute:

$$\text{Throughput} = 300 \, \frac{\text{cycles}}{\text{min}} \times 60 \, \frac{\text{min}}{\text{hr}}$$

$$\text{Throughput} = 18{,}000 \, \text{cycles/hour}$$

### Step 3: Account for Efficiency
At 100% efficiency:

$$\text{Effective Throughput} = 18{,}000 \times 1.00 = 18{,}000 \, \text{picks/hour}$$

> **Final Answer:** **18,000 picks per hour**

> [!NOTE]
> **Realistic Correction:** Industrial delta robots in production lines typically operate at **85–95% efficiency** due to vision-system delays, conveyor synchronization, and product infeed variability. So practical throughput is closer to **15,300 – 17,100 picks/hour**.

---

## 3.5 Case Study Walkthrough — Da Vinci Surgical System

**Background:** The Da Vinci Surgical System (Intuitive Surgical, USA) is a **teleoperated robotic surgical platform** used in over **10 million procedures worldwide** (as of 2024).

### Step 1: System Architecture
- **Master Console:** Surgeon sits at a 3-D HD display with hand and foot controls.
- **Patient Cart:** 4 robotic arms (1 endoscope + 3 instruments) are positioned over the patient.
- **Vision System:** 3-D high-definition endoscope with 10–15× magnification.
- **Instruments:** EndoWrist instruments with **7 degrees of freedom + gripper**.

### Step 2: Engineering Challenges Solved
- **Tremor Filtering:** Hand tremors of the surgeon (typically **8–12 Hz**) are filtered out by software, leaving only the intentional motion.
- **Motion Scaling:** Surgeon's hand movement of **5 cm** can be scaled to instrument movement of **1 cm** for micro-surgical precision.
- **Force Feedback (Haptics):** Newer systems (Da Vinci 5, 2024) introduce **force feedback**, a major leap from earlier purely-visual feedback.

### Step 3: Clinical Outcomes
- Reduced blood loss, shorter hospital stays, smaller scars, and faster patient recovery compared to open surgery.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Da Vinci system is the **gold standard** example for healthcare robotics in KTU exams. Be prepared to discuss its **kinematic structure, control architecture, and clinical advantages**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between an articulated robot and a SCARA robot in terms of joint configuration, workspace shape, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Articulated Robot | SCARA Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint Configuration** | 6 revolute joints in series | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic (vertical) |
| **Workspace Shape** | Approximately spherical | Cylindrical (short, flat) |
| **Typical DOF** | 6 | 4 |
| **Vertical Rigidity** | Low (compliant in all directions) | High (rigid vertically, compliant horizontally) |
| **Applications** | Welding, painting, heavy material handling | PCB assembly, pick-and-place, small-parts insertion |

**Conclusion:** Articulated robots are **versatile general-purpose manipulators**, while SCARA robots are **specialized for fast, precise vertical assembly** tasks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List any three key performance specifications used to evaluate an industrial robot. State the typical range of repeatability for a modern industrial robot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Key Specifications:**
1. **Payload (kg)** — Maximum mass the robot can carry at full extension.
2. **Reach (mm)** — Maximum distance from base to end-effector.
3. **Repeatability (mm)** — Deviation when returning to a previously taught position.

**Typical Repeatability Range:** Modern industrial robots exhibit repeatability in the range of **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**, with high-precision models (e.g., used in electronics) achieving **±0.005 mm**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of industrial robots based on **mechanical configuration**. Describe the structure, degrees of freedom, and typical applications of any three configurations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the application of robotics in the **healthcare sector** with at least **two detailed examples**, including system architecture and engineering challenges overcome.

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Industrial Robots by Mechanical Configuration:**

Industrial robots are classified into **six major types** based on the arrangement of their joints and the resulting kinematic structure.

**1. Articulated Robot (Jointed-Arm)**
- **Structure:** Consists of a rotating base, a shoulder, an elbow, and a 3-axis wrist — all revolute joints.
- **DOF:** Typically **6** (3 for positioning + 3 for orientation).
- **Applications:** Arc welding, spot welding, painting, material handling, machine tending.

**2. SCARA Robot**
- **Structure:** Two horizontal revolute joints + one vertical prismatic joint.
- **DOF:** Typically **4**.
- **Applications:** PCB assembly, pick-and-place of small components, screw-driving, dispensing.

**3. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot**
- **Structure:** Three prismatic joints along orthogonal X, Y, Z axes.
- **DOF:** **3** (extendable to 4–5 with a wrist).
- **Applications:** CNC loading/unloading, 3-D printing, palletizing, large-pick-and-place.

**4. Cylindrical Robot**
- **Structure:** One revolute + two prismatic joints.
- **Applications:** Foundry, die-casting, machine tending.

**5. Polar (Spherical) Robot**
- **Structure:** One revolute + one prismatic + one rotational.
- **Applications:** Forging, heavy material transfer.

**6. Delta (Parallel) Robot**
- **Structure:** Three parallelogram arms connected to a common platform.
- **Applications:** High-speed packaging (up to 300 picks/min), pharmaceutical sorting.

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The choice of configuration depends on **payload, reach, speed, and task geometry**, with articulated and SCARA robots being the most widely deployed in modern industry.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Robotics in Healthcare — Two Detailed Examples:**

**Example 1: Da Vinci Surgical System (Surgical Robotics)**

- **System Architecture:** Master-slave teleoperated system with three subsystems — master console (surgeon input), patient cart (4 robotic arms), and vision cart (3-D HD endoscope).
- **Engineering Challenges Overcome:**
  - **Tremor elimination** via software filtering of 8–12 Hz involuntary hand motion.
  - **Motion scaling** (5:1) for micro-precision.
  - **7-DOF EndoWrist instruments** that exceed human wrist dexterity inside the body.
  - **3-D HD vision** with 10–15× magnification.
- **Clinical Impact:** Over 10 million procedures performed; reduced patient trauma, faster recovery.

**Example 2: ReWalk Exoskeleton (Rehabilitation Robotics)**

- **System Architecture:** Wearable bionic exoskeleton with motorized hip and knee joints, tilt sensors, and a wrist-mounted controller.
- **Engineering Challenges Overcome:**
  - **Balance and gait stability** using real-time tilt sensors and microcontrollers.
  - **Battery-powered portability** for everyday use.
  - **FDA approval** ensuring clinical safety.
- **Clinical Impact:** Enables paraplegic patients to stand, walk, and climb stairs — over 1,000 users worldwide.

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Healthcare robotics represents one of the **fastest-growing segments** of the robotics industry, with the global market projected to exceed **$20 billion by 2027**, driven by aging populations and demand for precision surgery.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **current trends in robotics** with reference to **collaborative robots (cobots)**, **AI-driven manipulation**, and **humanoid robots**. Provide one industrial/commercial example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **case study**, explain how robotics is transforming **modern warehouse logistics** (e.g., Amazon's fulfillment centers). Discuss the key technologies involved and the engineering benefits achieved.

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Current Trends in Robotics:**

**1. Collaborative Robots (Cobots)**
- **Concept:** Cobots are designed to work **safely alongside humans** without the need for safety cages, using **force/torque sensing, speed limits, and rounded geometry** to prevent injury.
- **Example:** **Universal Robots UR10e** — a 6-DOF cobot with 12.5 kg payload, used in small-batch assembly, machine tending, and screw-driving in SME factories.
- **Key Advantage:** Lower integration cost, easier programming, flexible redeployment.

**2. AI-Driven Manipulation**
- **Concept:** Modern robots leverage **deep learning, computer vision, and large language models** to perceive, plan, and execute complex manipulation tasks.
- **Example:** **Google DeepMind's RT-2 (Robotics Transformer 2)** — a vision-language-action model that translates natural-language commands and visual inputs directly into robot actions without hand-coded rules.
- **Key Advantage:** Generalization to novel objects and tasks; reduced programming effort.

**3. Humanoid Robots**
- **Concept:** Bipedal robots with **human-like morphology**, designed to operate in human-built environments (stairs, doors, tools) without modification.
- **Example:** **Tesla Optimus (2024 prototype)** — a bipedal humanoid with 28+ actuators, designed for factory and domestic tasks.
- **Other Examples:** Boston Dynamics Atlas, Figure 01, Agility Robotics Digit.
- **Key Advantage:** Versatility in human-centric environments.

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The convergence of **AI, sensors, and mechatronics** is enabling a new generation of robots that are **smarter, safer, and more versatile** than their predecessors.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Case Study: Amazon's Robotic Warehouse Fulfillment System**

**Background:** Amazon operates over **750 robotic fulfillment centers** worldwide (as of 2024), with more than **1 million mobile robots** deployed.

**Key Technologies Involved:**

1. **Kiva (now Amazon Robotics) Mobile Robots**
   - Square-shaped orange robots that slide under shelves (pods) and lift them.
   - They navigate via **2-D floor fiducial markers (QR codes)** and centralized path planning.
   - Robots deliver the entire shelf to the human picker, eliminating the picker's walking time.

2. **Sparrow (Bin-Picking Robot)**
   - 6-DOF arm with **computer vision and suction grippers**.
   - Identifies and picks individual items from totes using deep-learning-based object recognition.
   - Handles **millions of distinct SKUs** in cluttered bins.

3. **Proteus (Autonomous Mobile Robot, 2023)**
   - First **fully autonomous** Amazon robot that can navigate around human workers.
   - Uses advanced sensors (LiDAR, depth cameras) for safe human-robot coexistence.

4. **Sequoia (Inventory Management, 2024)**
   - Integrates robots with **inventory storage**, enabling **75% faster inventory identification** and **25% faster order processing**.

**Engineering Benefits Achieved:**

| Benefit | Quantitative Impact |
|---|---|
| Order fulfillment time | Reduced from **60–75 min to ~15 min** |
| Warehouse storage density | Increased by **~40%** by eliminating fixed aisles |
| Walking distance per picker | Reduced by **~80%** |
| Operating cost | Reduced by **~20–25%** per order |
| Workplace injuries | Reduced through ergonomic shelf delivery |

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Amazon's robotic warehouse represents the **largest commercial deployment of mobile robots in history**, demonstrating how integration of mobile manipulators, AI vision, and centralized fleet management can transform logistics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy** — They are *not* the same. Repeatability is the *consistency* of returning to a taught point; accuracy is the *closeness to a computed* point. Many students lose 1–2 marks by not distinguishing them.
>
> 2. **Mixing up DOF and Axes** — A 6-DOF robot has 6 *independent* joints, not 6 axes. The "axes" terminology is informal; the rigorous term is "degrees of freedom."
>
> 3. **Naming the Wrong Domain for Case Studies** — If the question asks for *healthcare* robotics and you describe automotive welding, you receive **zero marks** for application, even if the rest is correct.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why"** — Merely listing applications (e.g., "robots are used in welding") is insufficient. KTU expects the **engineering justification** — *why* is a robot used? (Quality, safety, repeatability, cost.)
>
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams in 14-Mark Questions** — KTU explicitly rewards **neat, labeled diagrams**. A 14-mark question without a diagram typically loses **1–2 marks** even if the textual answer is perfect.
>
> 6. **Using "Robot" and "Machine Tool" Interchangeably** — A CNC machine is NOT a robot because it lacks versatility. This is a **favourite trick question** in KTU exams.
>
> 7. **Neglecting Units in Numerical Problems** — Always state units (m, mm, kg, m³) in your final answer to avoid losing a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- **Definition:** An industrial robot is a reprogrammable, multi-purpose manipulator with **≥ 3 axes** (ISO 8373:2021).
- **Six Mechanical Configurations:** Articulated, SCARA, Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, Delta (Mnemonic: **A-S-C-C-C-D**).
- **Key Metrics:** Payload (kg), Reach (mm), Repeatability (±mm), DOF, Workspace (m³), Cycle Time (s).
- **Repeatability vs Accuracy:** Repeatability = 3σ; Accuracy is *not* the same.
- **Workspace Formulas:** $V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$; $V_{cyl} = \pi R^{2} H$; $V_{box} = LWH$.
- **Manufacturing:** Articulated 6-DOF dominates (welding, painting, handling).
- **Healthcare:** Da Vinci (surgical), ReWalk (rehabilitation), pharmacy automation.
- **Service:** Cleaning (Roomba), delivery, warehouse (Amazon Kiva/Sparrow).
- **Agriculture:** Agrobot (harvesting), Ecorobotix (weeding).
- **Defense:** TALON (EOD), unmanned ground/aerial vehicles.
- **Space:** Canadarm2, Perseverance rover.
- **Current Trends:** **Cobots** (Universal Robots), **AI-driven manipulation** (RT-2), **Humanoid robots** (Optimus, Atlas), **Swarm robotics**, **Soft robotics**, **Haptics-enabled surgical robots**.
- **Case Studies to Memorize:** Da Vinci Surgical System, Amazon Kiva/Sparrow, Tesla Optimus, ReWalk Exoskeleton.
- **DOF Formula (Grübler–Kutzbach):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$.
- **Global Market:** Industrial robotics market projected to exceed **$80 billion by 2030**; collaborative robot segment growing at **>30% CAGR**.
- **Safety Standards:** ISO 10218 (industrial robots), ISO/TS 15066 (cobot safety), ISO 13482 (personal care robots).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human-Robot Interaction: Techniques and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human-Robot Interaction: Techniques and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)** is a multidisciplinary field of study dedicated to understanding, designing, and evaluating robotic systems for use by or with humans. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme definition, HRI encompasses the study of how humans interact with robots, encompassing the design of interfaces, communication protocols, safety mechanisms, and collaborative frameworks that enable seamless cooperation between human operators and robotic agents across diverse application domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** HRI is the intersection of robotics, cognitive psychology, ergonomics, human factors engineering, artificial intelligence, and human-computer interaction (HCI), focusing on the bidirectional communication and coordination between humans and robots in shared or collaborative workspaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are working alongside a **new intern** in a workshop. The intern is strong, tireless, and can lift heavy objects, but:
- The intern does not understand verbal sarcasm.
- The intern needs clear, structured instructions.
- The intern must be told where the "danger zones" are.
- The intern cannot guess what you are thinking — you must communicate explicitly.

A **robot** in a workplace behaves exactly like this intern. HRI is essentially the "**rulebook and language**" that allows the human supervisor (you) and the robotic worker (the intern) to work together safely, efficiently, and intuitively. The communication can be verbal, gestural, visual (screens), or even physical (force feedback).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HRI Matters in Industry 4.0 / 5.0:** Modern manufacturing floors (Industry 4.0) and human-centric automation (Industry 5.0) demand that humans and robots share workspace. Without robust HRI, the full potential of collaborative robotics (cobots) cannot be realized.

### 1.3 Core Metrics in HRI

The following measurable metrics are used to evaluate any HRI system:

- **Situation Awareness (SA):** The robot's awareness of human presence, intent, and state.
- **Task Efficiency:** Measured in **tasks completed per unit time** (typically **seconds/task**).
- **Cognitive Load:** The mental effort required from the human operator (measured via NASA-TLX index).
- **Trust Level:** Quantitative trust score, ranging from **0 (no trust)** to **1 (full trust)**.
- **Safety Index:** A composite metric based on **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative robots.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Bodies for HRI Norms:** **ISO 13482** (personal care robots), **ISO 10218** (industrial robots), and **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative robots) are the three pillars defining safety thresholds in HRI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of HRI

HRI is broadly classified into the following taxonomy:

- **Level 1 — Remote Interaction (Teleoperation):**
  - The human operates the robot from a distance.
  - Communication is unidirectional (commands) or bidirectional (force/video feedback).
  - *Example:* Bomb disposal robots, space rovers (NASA's **Curiosity**, **Perseverance**), underwater ROVs.

- **Level 2 — Proximate Interaction:**
  - Human and robot share the same physical workspace but do not necessarily cooperate.
  - *Example:* Industrial robotic arms in fenced cells, mobile robots in warehouses.

- **Level 3 — Interactive (Peer-to-Peer) Interaction:**
  - Human and robot engage in a coordinated, social, or collaborative manner.
  - *Example:* Cobots (e.g., **Universal Robots UR5e**), service robots in hospitals.

- **Level 4 — Full Social/Emotional Interaction:**
  - Robots interpret human emotions, gestures, and social cues.
  - *Example:* Companion robots (**Pepper**, **NAO**), therapeutic robots for autism therapy.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of HRI Techniques

HRI techniques can be grouped into three primary communication channels:

**Pillar 1 — Verbal / Auditory Communication**
- Speech recognition using **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** systems.
- Natural Language Processing (NLP) for intent extraction.
- Text-to-Speech (TTS) synthesis for robot responses.
- Challenges: Ambient noise, accent variability, latency.

**Pillar 2 — Non-Verbal / Visual Communication**
- Gestural recognition via **RGB-D cameras** (e.g., **Microsoft Kinect**, **Intel RealSense**).
- Facial expression analysis (using CNNs, OpenCV).
- Gaze tracking and body posture estimation.
- Visual displays (LEDs, tablets, AR glasses) for robot feedback.

**Pillar 3 — Physical / Haptic Communication**
- Force-sensitive skin and tactile sensors.
- Haptic joysticks providing force-feedback.
- **Impedance control** and **admittance control** for safe contact.
- Compliance control in cobots.

### 2.3 Collaborative Robot (Cobot) Safety Model

The **Kinematic Energy Limit** for safe human-robot contact (per **ISO/TS 15066**) is governed by:

$$E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $E_{kin}$ = kinetic energy of the moving robot link (**Joules, J**)
- $m$ = effective mass of the robot link at the contact point (**kg**)
- $v$ = relative velocity at the contact point (**m/s**)

The **Maximum Permissible Contact Pressure** ($P_{max}$) is also specified:

$$P_{max} = \frac{F_{contact}}{A_{contact}} \leq 50 \text{ N/cm}^2 \text{ (quasi-static, neck region)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this formula matters:** KTU frequently tests cobot safety. For a 14-mark question, students must demonstrate knowledge of both the energy limit AND the pressure limit, including the body region (head, neck, hand, etc.).

### 2.4 Trust Model in HRI (Quantitative)

The **Muir Trust Model** quantifies human trust in a robot as:

$$T(t) = T_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \Delta P_i(t)$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = trust level at time $t$ (range: **0 to 1**)
- $T_0$ = initial trust
- $w_i$ = weighting factor for performance attribute $i$
- $\Delta P_i(t)$ = change in performance attribute $i$ at time $t$
- $n$ = total number of observable performance attributes

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Units / Standard Value** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Cobot Kinetic Energy | $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) | Safety threshold for HRI |
| Contact Pressure | $P = F / A$ | N/cm² (limit: **50**) | ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static |
| Stopping Distance (Cobot) | $d = v \cdot t_{reaction} + \frac{v^2}{2a}$ | meters (m) | Power and Force Limiting (PFL) |
| Trust Score | $T \in [0, 1]$ | dimensionless | Muir Model |
| Reaction Time (Human) | $t_r \approx 0.25$ to **0.4 s** | seconds | ISO 13849 safety distance |
| Deceleration (Cobot) | $a = 2.5$ m/s² (quasi-static) | m/s² | Collaborative operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When asked about "safe human-robot coexistence", always quote **ISO/TS 15066** along with the **kinetic energy limit** formula. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for citing the standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Safe Distance for Collaborative Robot

In a collaborative workspace, the robot must come to a complete stop before a human enters the danger zone. The **minimum protective distance** $S$ is derived as follows.

**Given:**
- Robot's approach velocity: $v = 0.5$ m/s
- Human's walking speed: $v_h = 1.6$ m/s
- Robot's reaction time: $t_{rx} = 0.1$ s
- Robot's braking deceleration: $a = 2.5$ m/s²
- Human's reach into the workspace: $d_h = 0.5$ m

**Step 1 — Braking Distance of the Robot**

The robot, upon detecting a human, needs distance equal to its braking travel.

$$d_{brake} = v \cdot t_{rx} + \frac{v^2}{2a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$d_{brake} = 0.5 \cdot 0.1 + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2 \cdot 2.5}$$

$$d_{brake} = 0.05 + \frac{0.25}{5}$$

$$d_{brake} = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Human's reach time**

The human, moving at $v_h$, takes time $t_h$ to traverse the reach $d_h$.

$$t_h = \frac{d_h}{v_h} = \frac{0.5}{1.6} = 0.3125 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Total safety distance**

The total safe separation is the sum of the robot's braking distance and the human's reach, ensuring the robot is fully halted before the human reaches the hazardous region.

$$S_{total} = d_{brake} + (v_h \cdot t_h)$$

$$S_{total} = 0.10 + (1.6 \cdot 0.3125)$$

$$S_{total} = 0.10 + 0.50 = 0.60 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The minimum safe separation distance is **$S_{total} = 0.60 \text{ m}$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this derivation is important:** This is a KTU-favorite 7-mark sub-question. The expected answer structure is: state the standard (ISO 13855), show the formula, substitute values, and conclude with units.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Trust Score Evolution

**Problem:** A surgical robot performs 10 operations. Initially, trust $T_0 = 0.5$. After 3 successful operations (weight $w_1 = 0.3$, performance gain $\Delta P_1 = +0.2$), and 1 minor error ($w_2 = 0.4$, performance loss $\Delta P_2 = -0.1$), calculate the final trust score.

**Step 1 — Identify the Muir Model**

$$T = T_0 + \sum_{i} w_i \cdot \Delta P_i$$

**Step 2 — Expand the summation**

$$T = T_0 + (w_1 \cdot \Delta P_1) + (w_2 \cdot \Delta P_2)$$

$$T = 0.5 + (0.3 \cdot 0.2) + (0.4 \cdot -0.1)$$

$$T = 0.5 + 0.06 - 0.04$$

$$T = 0.52$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The final trust score is **0.52**, slightly above initial trust, indicating the human operator remains cautiously optimistic about the robot.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: HRI Techniques vs Applications

| **HRI Technique** | **Sensor / Input** | **Algorithm** | **Typical Application** | **Latency Tolerance** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| Speech Recognition | Microphone array | HMM, RNN, Transformer | Service robots, home assistants | **< 500 ms** |
| Gesture Recognition | RGB-D Camera | CNN + LSTM | Industrial teaching, sign-language robots | **< 100 ms** |
| Gaze Tracking | IR Eye Tracker | Pupil detection | Wheelchair robots, teleoperation | **< 50 ms** |
| Haptic Feedback | Force sensors | Impedance control | Surgical robots (**Da Vinci**) | **< 10 ms** |
| Brain-Computer Interface | EEG Cap | Motor imagery decoding | Assistive robots for paralysis | **< 200 ms** |
| Tactile Skin | Capacitive/Resistive grid | Spike-based processing | Prosthetic limbs, cobot arms | **< 5 ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the inverse relationship between **latency tolerance** and **safety criticality**. Surgical and prosthetic applications demand sub-10ms response, whereas home assistants tolerate 500ms. This tradeoff is a common exam topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: HRI Communication Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Human Operator] -->|Intent / Command| B[Perception Layer]
    B --> C[Modality 1 Speech Recognition]
    B --> D[Modality 2 Gesture Vision]
    B --> E[Modality 3 Haptic Touch]
    C --> F[Fusion Engine]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Intent Decoder NLP]
    G --> H[Task Planner]
    H --> I[Safety Supervisor ISO 15066]
    I --> J[Robot Actuator Controller]
    J --> K[Physical Robot Manipulator]
    K -->|Force Feedback| L[Haptic Display]
    K -->|Visual Status| M[LED / Screen]
    L --> A
    M --> A
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#9BC4F5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#F58C9B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Information flows **from human → perception → fusion → planning → safety → robot → feedback → human**. The **Safety Supervisor (red block)** is the ISO 15066 gatekeeper that can override all commands to ensure human well-being.

### 4.2 HRI Levels Taxonomy (Yaxis Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1 Teleoperation] --> L2[Level 2 Proximate]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Interactive Collaborative]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Social Emotional]
    L1 --> L1A[Example Mars Rover]
    L2 --> L2A[Example Industrial Cell]
    L3 --> L3A[Example UR5e Cobot]
    L4 --> L4A[Example Pepper Robot]
    style L1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L3 fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L4 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Multimodal Fusion State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Listening: Voice Activated
    Idle --> Watching: Motion Detected
    Listening --> Confirming: NLP Intent Confirmed
    Watching --> Confirming: Gesture Classified
    Confirming --> Executing: Safety Check Passed
    Confirming --> Aborted: Ambiguous or Unsafe
    Executing --> FeedbackActive: Task In Progress
    FeedbackActive --> Idle: Task Complete
    Aborted --> Idle: Reset
    FeedbackActive --> EmergencyStop: Force Overload
    EmergencyStop --> Idle: Manual Reset
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "draw the HRI block diagram" in an exam, always include the **Feedback Loop** from robot to human. Diagrams without feedback are marked down 1–2 marks. The safety/emergency branch is a bonus differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). List any four major application areas of HRI.**

**Model Answer:**
Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) is the multidisciplinary field of study focused on the design, development, and evaluation of robotic systems that communicate and collaborate with humans through verbal, non-verbal, and physical modalities.

**Four major application areas:**
1. **Industrial manufacturing** (cobot assembly lines, e.g., BMW and Universal Robots).
2. **Medical and surgical robotics** (e.g., Da Vinci Surgical System).
3. **Service and domestic robotics** (e.g., vacuum cleaners, elderly care robots).
4. **Space and underwater exploration** (e.g., NASA's Perseverance rover, deep-sea ROVs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** Examiners expect 4 distinct domains. Avoid repeating sub-applications of the same domain. Mentioning a real product/robot name adds 0.5 bonus mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is a "Cobot"? Mention the safety standard applicable to collaborative robots.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Cobot** (collaborative robot) is a robot designed for direct physical interaction with a human operator within a shared, defined workspace, without the need for traditional safety cages or fences.

The applicable safety standard is **ISO/TS 15066:2016**, which supplements **ISO 10218-1/2** and defines four collaborative operation modes:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)**
2. **Hand Guiding (HG)**
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)**
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the different levels of Human-Robot Interaction as defined by the Goodrich & Schultz taxonomy. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 7-DOF collaborative robot arm has an effective mass of **$m = 8$ kg** at the end-effector. The maximum allowable contact velocity is **$v = 0.25$ m/s** for the hand region. Calculate the maximum permissible kinetic energy and verify whether it complies with **ISO/TS 15066** (limit: **$E_{max} = 1.7$ J** for the hand). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Goodrich & Schultz (2007)** taxonomy classifies HRI into **four levels**, progressing from remote to fully social:

1. **Level 1 – Remote Interaction (Teleoperation):**
   - Human controls a distant robot.
   - Applications: space exploration, bomb disposal, telesurgery.

2. **Level 2 – Proximate Interaction:**
   - Human and robot share a workspace, but interaction is limited.
   - Applications: industrial robots, mobile service robots.

3. **Level 3 – Interactive (Peer-to-Peer):**
   - Active cooperation on a shared task.
   - Applications: cobots, rehabilitation robots.

4. **Level 4 – Social/Emotional Interaction:**
   - Robots interpret and respond to human emotions.
   - Applications: companion robots, autism therapy, elderly care.

The block diagram should depict the **progression from physical to social coupling**, with increasing reliance on **AI and cognitive modeling** at higher levels.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — State the kinetic energy formula**

$$E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**

$$E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8 \cdot (0.25)^2$$

$$E_{kin} = 4 \cdot 0.0625$$

$$E_{kin} = 0.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ISO/TS 15066 limit**

$$E_{kin} = 0.25 \text{ J} \leq E_{max} = 1.7 \text{ J} \quad \text{(for hand region)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

The computed kinetic energy of **0.25 J** is **well within** the permissible limit of **1.7 J** for the hand region. The cobot operation is **safe and compliant** with ISO/TS 15066 standards.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the three primary communication modalities in HRI with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **multimodal fusion** in improving interaction robustness. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A warehouse cobot moves at a constant velocity of **$v = 0.8$ m/s** toward a human worker. The cobot's reaction time is **$t_{rx} = 0.2$ s** and its braking deceleration is **$a = 3.0$ m/s²**. Calculate the **braking distance** of the cobot and the **minimum safe separation** if the human's walking speed is **$v_h = 1.5$ m/s** and reach is **$d_h = 0.4$ m**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The three primary modalities of HRI are:

1. **Verbal / Auditory Modality:**
   - Uses **ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition)** and **NLP** to decode spoken commands.
   - *Example:* Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant controlling smart-home robots.

2. **Non-Verbal / Visual Modality:**
   - Uses **RGB-D cameras** and **CNNs** to recognize gestures, facial expressions, and gaze.
   - *Example:* Kinect-based gesture teaching for industrial robots.

3. **Physical / Haptic Modality:**
   - Uses **force/torque sensors** and **tactile skin** to perceive physical contact.
   - *Example:* Hand-guiding a cobot by physically pushing its arm.

**Role of Multimodal Fusion:**

Multimodal fusion combines two or more modalities (e.g., voice + gesture) using algorithms such as **Kalman filters, Bayesian networks, or deep learning-based sensor fusion**. It improves robustness by:
- Compensating for **sensor noise** in any single modality.
- Resolving **ambiguity** (e.g., "place it there" + pointing gesture).
- Increasing **reliability** in adverse conditions (noisy factory, dim lighting).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Braking Distance Formula**

$$d_{brake} = v \cdot t_{rx} + \frac{v^2}{2a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$d_{brake} = 0.8 \cdot 0.2 + \frac{(0.8)^2}{2 \cdot 3.0}$$

$$d_{brake} = 0.16 + \frac{0.64}{6.0}$$

$$d_{brake} = 0.16 + 0.1067 = 0.2667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Human's reach time**

$$t_h = \frac{d_h}{v_h} = \frac{0.4}{1.5} = 0.2667 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Safe Separation**

$$S_{min} = d_{brake} + (v_h \cdot t_h)$$

$$S_{min} = 0.2667 + (1.5 \cdot 0.2667)$$

$$S_{min} = 0.2667 + 0.4000 = 0.6667 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The cobot will stop after **0.27 m**, and the minimum safe separation is approximately **$S_{min} \approx 0.67$ m**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reaction-time term:** Many students omit $v \cdot t_{rx}$ and lose 2 marks. Always include the full formula.
> 2. **Wrong units:** Energy must be in **Joules (J)**, not "Joules per second". Distance in **meters**, not "centimeters".
> 3. **Skipping the standard:** Quoting "ISO/TS 15066" without naming the specific limit (e.g., **1.7 J for hand**) loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing the four cobot modes:** Memorize **SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL** in the correct order — examiners test this directly.
> 5. **Ignoring the feedback loop:** In block diagrams, missing the **robot-to-human feedback** path is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **HRI Definition:** Multidisciplinary field; design of communication between humans and robots.
- **Goodrich & Schultz Levels:** **4 levels** — Teleoperation, Proximate, Interactive, Social.
- **Cobot:** Collaborative robot; no safety cage; works alongside humans.
- **Key Safety Standard:** **ISO/TS 15066** (supplements ISO 10218).
- **Four Collaborative Modes:** **SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL**.
- **Kinetic Energy Formula:** $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ — must be ≤ ISO limit (e.g., **1.7 J for hand**).
- **Contact Pressure Limit:** **50 N/cm²** (quasi-static, neck region).
- **Stopping Distance Formula:** $d = v \cdot t_{rx} + \frac{v^2}{2a}$ — used in Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM).
- **Three Communication Modalities:** **Verbal (ASR/NLP)**, **Non-verbal (Vision/Gesture)**, **Haptic (Force/Tactile)**.
- **Multimodal Fusion:** Combines ≥2 modalities; improves robustness via redundancy.
- **Trust Model:** Muir Model — $T \in [0, 1]$; increases with positive performance, decreases with errors.
- **Key HRI Applications:** **Industrial cobots, surgical robots, service robots, space rovers, assistive/prosthetic devices, BCI-driven robots**.
- **Standards to Remember:** **ISO 13482** (personal care), **ISO 10218** (industrial), **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative), **ISO 13855** (safety distances).
- **Example Robots for Naming:** **UR5e** (cobot), **Da Vinci** (surgery), **Pepper** (social), **Perseverance** (space), **ASIMO** (Honda humanoid).
- **Safety Braking Distance Numericals:** Always include **reaction time**; final answer in **meters** with **2 decimal places**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for HRI Module:**
> 1. For **3-mark questions** — quote a standard (ISO/TS 15066) and a numeric value.
> 2. For **7-mark derivations** — show all four steps: formula → substitution → computation → interpretation.
> 3. For **7-mark theory** — use the **classification table** (level / modality / application) and pair it with a **neat Mermaid-style block diagram**.
> 4. Always conclude with a **practical example** (UR5e, Da Vinci, Pepper) — examiners reward real-world context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Robots: Navigation and Decision-Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT722 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Robots: Navigation and Decision-Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Autonomous Robot

An **autonomous robot** is a mechanical, electromechanical, or computational entity capable of performing tasks in real-world environments **without continuous human guidance**, by perceiving its surroundings, reasoning about them, localizing itself, planning a course of action, and executing motion commands in a closed feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** An autonomous robot is one that exhibits the three classical properties of *autonomy*: (1) **self-governance** (no external teleoperation), (2) **situational awareness** (real-time perception), and (3) **adaptive decision-making** (closed-loop reactive behavior in response to dynamic obstacles).

### 1.2 Navigation: Formal Definition

**Robot Navigation** is the science and engineering of determining (a) *where the robot is* (the **localization** problem), (b) *where it wants to go* (the **goal specification**), and (c) *how to get there safely* (the **path / motion planning** problem), while continuously updating all three using onboard sensors.

### 1.3 Decision-Making: Formal Definition

**Decision-Making** in robotics is the cognitive process of **selecting an optimal action** $a \in \mathcal{A}$ from a discrete or continuous action space, based on the current state estimate $s$, the perceived environment map $m$, and an objective (cost / reward) function $J(s,a)$. Formally:

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; J(s, a)$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Self-Driving Car as a "Robot on Wheels":** Imagine you are driving from your college in Thrissur to the Technopark in Trivandrum for an internship. You (a) check Google Maps to know **where you are** and **where you must reach** (localization + goal), (b) plan the shortest NH-66 route avoiding city traffic (global path planning), (c) reactively steer away from a suddenly cutting autorickshaw (local obstacle avoidance), and (d) decide whether to overtake or stay behind (decision-making). An autonomous robot performs **exactly** these four jobs — but using LIDAR, encoders, IMUs, and algorithms instead of human eyes and instinct.

### 1.5 Core Sub-Problems (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| Sub-Problem | Function | Classical Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Mapping** | Build a representation $m$ of the world | Occupancy Grid, SLAM |
| **Localization** | Estimate robot pose $x = (x, y, \theta)$ | Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Monte-Carlo Localization |
| **Global Path Planning** | Compute optimal route from start to goal | A*, Dijkstra, RRT, PRM |
| **Local Obstacle Avoidance** | Reactive collision-free motion | Vector Field Histogram, Dynamic Window Approach |
| **Decision-Making** | Choose high-level behavior | FSM, Behavior Trees, POMDP, RL |

### 1.6 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Path Optimality** $\eta$ — ratio of actual path length to optimal length, ideally **≈ 1.0**.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** of localization $\sigma^2_{xy}$ — ideally $< 0.1 \text{ m}^2$ for indoor robots.
- **Cycle Time** $T_c$ — between **10 Hz and 100 Hz** for mobile platforms.
- **Safety Clearance** $d_{safe} \geq 0.3 \text{ m}$ for indoor service robots.
- **Speed of Sound** $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$ — used in ultrasonic time-of-flight sensors.
- **Speed of Light** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ — used in LIDAR ranging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classical Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Architecture

The earliest and most pedagogically important paradigm, introduced by **Nilsson (1984)** in the Stanford Cart and Shakey the Robot projects, decomposes autonomy into three sequential stages:

1. **Sense** — Acquire raw sensor data $z_t$ from LIDAR, camera, IMU, wheel encoders.
2. **Plan** — Build / update a world model $m_t$, localize, then generate a path $\pi$.
3. **Act** — Convert path $\pi$ into low-level motor commands $u_t$ (velocities, torques).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Variant — Hybrid Deliberative / Reactive (sense–plan–act in parallel):** Real KTU-board-recommended robots run global planning at **1–5 Hz** and reactive obstacle avoidance at **20–100 Hz** simultaneously. The lower layer *overrides* the upper layer only during imminent collision (time-to-collision $TTC < 1.5$ s).

### 2.2 Robot Localization — The Recursive Bayes Filter

All modern localization algorithms are special cases of the **Recursive Bayesian Filter**, which maintains a probability distribution $\text{bel}(x_t)$ over the robot pose $x_t$:

**Prediction step** (using odometry $u_t$ and motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$):

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Measurement update step** (using sensor observation $z_t$ and model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$):

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring the distribution integrates to 1. The **Kalman Filter** is the closed-form Gaussian solution; the **Particle Filter** is the non-parametric, multi-modal solution.

### 2.3 A* Path Planning — The Heuristic Search Backbone

A* finds the shortest path on a discretized grid $G = (V, E)$ by combining:

- **g(n)** — actual cost from start to node $n$.
- **h(n)** — admissible heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal.
- **f(n) = g(n) + h(n)** — total estimated cost through $n$.

The algorithm expands the node with **minimum f(n)** first, guaranteeing **optimality** if $h(n)$ is admissible (never overestimates).

### 2.4 Artificial Potential Field Method (Reactive Layer)

Invented by **Khatib (1986)**, this method treats the robot as a point under the influence of:

- An **attractive** potential pulling it toward the goal.
- A **repulsive** potential pushing it away from obstacles.

The total force is the negative gradient of total potential — a direct application of classical mechanics in configuration space.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving Module-4 numericals in the KTU University Exam. Memorize the boxed units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* evaluation function | dimensionless |
| 2 | $h_{Manhattan}(n) = \vert x_g - x_n \vert + \vert y_g - y_n \vert$ | Admissible heuristic (4-neighbour grid) | m |
| 3 | $h_{Euclid}(n) = \sqrt{(x_g-x_n)^2 + (y_g-y_n)^2}$ | Admissible heuristic (8-neighbour grid) | m |
| 4 | $L_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$ | Total Euclidean path length | m |
| 5 | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$ | Conical + parabolic attractive potential | J |
| 6 | $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ if $\rho \le \rho_0$, else 0 | Repulsive potential of one obstacle | J |
| 7 | $\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$ | Resultant control force | N |
| 8 | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B u_k$ | KF prediction (state) | m, m/s, rad |
| 9 | $P_{k\mid k-1} = A P_{k-1\mid k-1} A^{T} + Q$ | KF prediction (covariance) | varies |
| 10 | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T}\!\left( H P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T} + R \right)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | KF update (state) | m, m/s, rad |
| 12 | $TTC = \dfrac{d}{\vert v_{rel} \vert}$ | Time-to-collision (reactive safety) | s |
| 13 | $d_{LIDAR} = \dfrac{c\, \Delta t}{2}$ | LIDAR range via time-of-flight | m |
| 14 | $d_{sonar} = \dfrac{c_{sound}\, \Delta t}{2}$ | Sonar range via time-of-flight | m |
| 15 | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{optimal}}$ | Path optimality ratio ($\eta \ge 1$) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations (1)–(4) drive *global planning* in warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva bots). Equations (5)–(7) drive *reactive obstacle avoidance* in service robots like the SoftBank Pepper. Equations (8)–(11) drive the *sensor fusion core* of every self-driving car, drone (PX4 autopilot), and even the Mars rovers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient of the Attractive Potential Field

We wish to find the attractive force pulling the robot from configuration $q = (x, y)$ toward the goal $q_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)$.

**Step 1.** Define the Euclidean distance to the goal:

$$\rho_{goal}(q) = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}}$$

**Step 2.** Define the attractive potential energy as a quadratic function of distance (a parabolic well in configuration space):

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

where $k_{att} > 0$ is the tunable **attractive gain** [N/m].

**Step 3.** The attractive force is the **negative spatial gradient** of potential energy (conservative force field):

$$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x}\,\hat{i} - \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y}\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 4.** Compute partial derivative w.r.t. $x$. Using the chain rule and the fact that $\frac{\partial \rho_{goal}}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_g}{\rho_{goal}}$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\cdot 2\rho_{goal}\cdot \frac{\partial \rho_{goal}}{\partial x} = k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}\cdot \frac{x - x_g}{\rho_{goal}} = k_{att}(x - x_g)$$

**Step 5.** Similarly, partial derivative w.r.t. $y$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = k_{att}(y - y_g)$$

**Step 6.** Assemble the final vector result:

$$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -k_{att}\!\left[ (x - x_g)\,\hat{i} + (y - y_g)\,\hat{j} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The attractive force is a **linear restoring spring** anchored at the goal. The further the robot is, the stronger the pull. This is exactly Hooke's law applied to robot motion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of the Repulsive Potential Field (Khatib Form)

We want a force that grows **inversely** with distance to the nearest obstacle and is **zero** beyond influence radius $\rho_0$.

**Step 1.** Let $\rho(q)$ be the shortest distance from robot to the nearest obstacle surface.

**Step 2.** Define the repulsive potential (zero outside influence zone):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\!\left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3.** Compute partial derivative w.r.t. $x$. Let $\phi(q) = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep}\,\phi(q)\cdot \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\partial \phi / \partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = -\frac{k_{rep}}{\rho^{2}(q)}\!\left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6.** Repulsive force in vector form:

$$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\!\left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)}\, \nabla \rho(q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This repulsive force profile ensures that *as $\rho \to 0$* (collision imminent), the force $F_{rep} \to \infty$, guaranteeing mathematically that the robot never penetrates an obstacle. It is the reason why the potential-field method is **formally complete** for static environments.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — A* Search on a 4×4 Grid

**Given problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** Robot must travel from **S = (0,0)** to **G = (3,3)** on the 4×4 grid shown below. Walls exist at cells (1,2) and (2,1). Use A* with **Manhattan heuristic**. Movement is 4-connected (up/down/left/right, cost = 1 per step).

**Step 1 — Initialize.** Set $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + (3+3) = 6$. Open set $\mathcal{O} = \{S\}$.

**Step 2 — Expand S.** Neighbours: (1,0) and (0,1).

- For (1,0): $g = 1$, $h = \vert 3-1\vert + \vert 3-0\vert = 2+3 = 5$, so $f = 1+5 = 6$.
- For (0,1): $g = 1$, $h = \vert 3-0\vert + \vert 3-1\vert = 3+2 = 5$, so $f = 1+5 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Expand (0,1) (ties broken by smaller g).** Neighbours: (0,2) and (1,1) [since (0,0) is closed].

- For (0,2): $g = 2$, $h = 3+1 = 4$, $f = 2+4 = 6$.
- For (1,1): $g = 2$, $h = 2+2 = 4$, $f = 2+4 = 6$.

**Step 4 — Expand (1,0).** Neighbours: (1,1), (2,0).

- (1,1) already known with $g=2$; new path gives $g = 1+1 = 2$ — same, so keep.
- (2,0): $g = 2$, $h = 1+3 = 4$, $f = 2+4 = 6$.

**Step 5 — Continue expanding lowest-f nodes.** Following the standard A* tie-breaking, the algorithm eventually reaches G with $g(G) = 6$.

**Step 6 — Optimal path length:**

$$L_{path} = 6 \text{ cells} \quad \text{(e.g., S} \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3) \text{)}$$

**Step 7 — Optimality check.** Manhattan distance is 6, so $\eta = L_{actual}/L_{optimal} = 6/6 = 1.0$ — **optimal**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Kalman Filter 1-D Position Tracking

**Given:** A robot moves along x-axis. State = position only (constant velocity not tracked for simplicity). Process model: $x_k = x_{k-1} + w_k$ with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ where $Q = 0.1$. Measurement model: $z_k = x_k + v_k$ with $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ where $R = 1.0$. Initial: $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1$. First measurement: $z_1 = 2.0$.

**Step 1 — Prediction (k = 1):** $A = 1$, $B = 0$ (no control), $H = 1$.

$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = A\hat{x}_0 = 1 \times 0 = 0$$

$$P_{1\mid 0} = A P_0 A^{T} + Q = 1\times 1\times 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

**Step 2 — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = P_{1\mid 0} H^{T}\!\left( H P_{1\mid 0} H^{T} + R \right)^{-1} = 1.1 \times (1.1 + 1.0)^{-1} = \frac{1.1}{2.1} \approx 0.524$$

**Step 3 — State Update:**

$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 1} = \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} + K_1(z_1 - H\hat{x}_{1\mid 0}) = 0 + 0.524 \times (2.0 - 0) = 1.048$$

**Step 4 — Covariance Update:**

$$P_{1\mid 1} = (1 - K_1 H)P_{1\mid 0} = (1 - 0.524)\times 1.1 = 0.524$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice $P$ decreased from $1.0 \to 0.524$ — the filter is *more confident* after incorporating the measurement. This contraction of uncertainty is the essence of recursive estimation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Autonomous Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Sensors LIDAR Camera IMU Encoders GPS] --> P1[Perception Module Sensor Fusion and Occupancy Grid]
    P1 --> LK[Localization Module Kalman Filter or Particle Filter]
    P1 --> MP[Mapping Module SLAM]
    LK --> WM[World Model State plus Map]
    MP --> WM
    WM --> GP[Global Planner A Star or RRT]
    GP --> PP[Path]
    P1 --> RA[Reactive Layer Potential Field or VFH]
    PP --> ARB[Arbiter Supervisor FSM]
    RA --> ARB
    ARB --> CL[Controller PID or MPC]
    CL --> A1[Actuators Wheel Motors Steering Servos]
    A1 --> ENV[Environment]
    ENV --> S1
    WM --> DM[Decision Making Layer Behavior Tree or POMDP]
    DM --> ARB
    classDef sensor fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef process fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef act fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class S1,A1,ENV sensor
    class P1,LK,MP,WM,GP,PP,RA,ARB,CL,DM process
    class CL,A1 act
```

### 4.2 Decision-Making Hierarchy using Finite State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Exploring : Goal Received
    Exploring --> Planning : Map Updated
    Planning --> Executing : Path Found
    Executing --> Avoiding : Obstacle Detected within 0.3m
    Avoiding --> Executing : Path Clear
    Executing --> Replanning : Path Blocked
    Replanning --> Planning : New Path Computed
    Executing --> GoalReached : At Goal
    Avoiding --> GoalReached : At Goal
    GoalReached --> Idle : Task Done
    Executing --> EmergencyStop : Battery Low or Imminent Collision
    Avoiding --> EmergencyStop : Sensor Failure
    EmergencyStop --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Data Flow during a Single Navigation Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sens as Sensors
    participant Fuse as Fusion Engine
    participant Loc as Localization
    participant Plan as Global Planner
    participant React as Reactive Avoidance
    participant Ctrl as Motor Controller
    Sens->>Fuse: Raw z_t LIDAR plus IMU plus GPS
    Fuse->>Loc: Cleaned state measurement
    Loc->>Plan: Updated pose hat x_t
    Plan->>Ctrl: Reference trajectory pi
    React-->>Ctrl: Reactive velocity override
    Ctrl->>Sens: Wheel commands u_t
    Note over Sens,Ctrl: Cycle repeats at 20 to 100 Hz
```

### 4.4 Map and Path Output (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start S at origin] --> B[Node 1 at 1,0]
    B --> C[Node 2 at 2,0]
    C --> D[Node 3 at 3,0]
    D --> E[Node 4 at 3,1]
    E --> F[Node 5 at 3,2]
    F --> G[Goal G at 3,3]
    H((Obstacle at 1,2)) -.->|avoided| B
    I((Obstacle at 2,1)) -.->|avoided| C
    classDef goal fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef obst fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class G goal
    class H,I obst
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the term *autonomous robot*. List the three essential capabilities it must possess.**

**Model Answer:**
An *autonomous robot* is a robot capable of performing its assigned tasks in unstructured environments **without continuous human intervention**, by perceiving, reasoning, and acting in a closed feedback loop. The three essential capabilities (per KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

1. **Self-localization** — knowing its own pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world.
2. **Path planning** — computing a collision-free route from start to goal.
3. **Reactive obstacle avoidance** — modifying its motion in real time to handle unforeseen obstacles.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + ½ each capability × 3 = 1.5, rounded up)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Differentiate between *global path planning* and *local obstacle avoidance* in mobile robots.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Global Planning | Local Avoidance |
|---|---|---|
| **Time horizon** | Long (entire mission) | Short (next 1–2 seconds) |
| **Map required** | Full prior map | Only local sensor window |
| **Algorithm** | A*, Dijkstra, RRT, PRM | Potential field, VFH, DWA |
| **Update rate** | 1–5 Hz | 20–100 Hz |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal path | Only locally optimal |
| **Failure mode** | Computationally expensive | Can get stuck in local minima |

*(3 marks: 1 for table + 1 for example + 1 for difference)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Path Planning & Localization

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** *Explain the A* path-planning algorithm with the cost function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. State the admissibility condition for the heuristic. Using A* on the 5×5 grid given below (S = start at (0,0), G = goal at (4,4), walls at (1,1), (1,3), (3,2)), compute the optimal path and its total length using the **Manhattan heuristic** and 4-connected movement.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the state-prediction and measurement-update equations of the **Linear Kalman Filter** for a 1-D mobile robot tracking problem. Clearly state the role of the process noise covariance $Q$ and measurement noise covariance $R$. If $Q = 0.1$ and $R = 4.0$, comment on whether the filter trusts the model more or the measurement more.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — A* Overview:** A* is a best-first search algorithm that combines the actual cost $g(n)$ from start to node $n$ and the heuristic estimate $h(n)$ from $n$ to goal. The node with the **lowest $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$** is expanded first. **Admissibility condition:** $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all $n$, i.e., the heuristic must never overestimate the true cost. The Manhattan distance is admissible on a 4-neighbour grid.

**Step 2 — Initialize:** $\mathcal{O} = \{(0,0)\}$, $f(0,0) = 0 + (4+4) = 8$.

**Step 3 — Expand (0,0):** Neighbours (1,0) and (0,1), each with $g = 1$. Heuristics:

- $h(1,0) = 3+4 = 7 \Rightarrow f = 8$
- $h(0,1) = 4+3 = 7 \Rightarrow f = 8$

**Step 4 — Continue Expansion:** Following the standard A* process and avoiding the wall cells, one valid optimal trajectory is:

$$S(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (4,0) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4)=G$$

**Step 5 — Compute Length:** Number of cells traversed = 8 steps, hence $L = 8$ units. Since the Manhattan distance from S to G is 8, optimality ratio $\eta = 8/8 = 1.0$.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — System Model:** Linear discrete-time system:

$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

$$z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Step 2 — Prediction (a priori estimate):**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B u_k$$

$$P_{k\mid k-1} = A P_{k-1\mid k-1} A^{T} + Q$$

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T}\!\left( H P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T} + R \right)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Update (a posteriori estimate):**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$$

$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Q and R:**

- $Q$ models **process noise** — uncertainty in motion (wheel slip, terrain roughness). Large $Q \Rightarrow$ filter trusts the *model less*, gives more weight to measurements.
- $R$ models **sensor noise** — uncertainty in observation. Large $R \Rightarrow$ filter trusts the *measurement less*, relies more on the motion model.

**Step 6 — Numerical Comment:** Given $Q = 0.1$ and $R = 4.0$, we have $R \gg Q$ (40× larger). Therefore the filter is **highly sceptical of the sensor** and relies predominantly on the dead-reckoning motion model. The result is a **smoother but less accurate** estimate — typical of low-cost IMU / encoder fusion in low-cost wheeled robots.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Reactive Methods & Decision-Making

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** *Explain the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method for robot navigation. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive forces for a point robot in a 2-D workspace containing one circular obstacle of radius $r_o$ centred at $(x_o, y_o)$. State two disadvantages of the APF method.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare **Finite State Machines (FSM)**, **Behavior Trees (BT)**, and **POMDP-based decision-making** for autonomous robots. State one real-world application where each is most appropriate.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Concept:** In APF (Khatib, 1986), the robot is treated as a charged particle moving in a potential field $U(q)$ where the goal is an *attractor* and obstacles are *repulsors*. Motion follows the negative gradient:

$$\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

**Step 2 — Attractive Field and Force:** With $\rho_{goal}^{2} = (x-x_g)^{2} + (y-y_g)^{2}$:

$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

$$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -k_{att}\!\left[ (x-x_g)\hat{i} + (y-y_g)\hat{j} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Repulsive Field:** Distance from robot to obstacle surface:

$$\rho(q) = \sqrt{(x-x_o)^2 + (y-y_o)^2} - r_o$$

The repulsive potential (active only within influence radius $\rho_0$):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\!\left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive Force:** Differentiating,

$$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\!\left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \nabla \rho(q)$$

where $\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{(x-x_o)\hat{i} + (y-y_o)\hat{j}}{\sqrt{(x-x_o)^2 + (y-y_o)^2}}$.

**Step 5 — Two Disadvantages:**

1. **Local Minima Trap:** When the attractive and repulsive forces cancel exactly at a non-goal point, the robot stops prematurely.
2. **Oscillation in Narrow Corridors:** The robot zig-zags between two opposing repulsive forces near closely-spaced obstacles, leading to unstable motion.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

| Method | Structure | Strengths | Weaknesses | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Finite State Machine (FSM)** | Discrete states with deterministic transitions | Simple, deterministic, easy to verify | State explosion; poor reactivity | Roomba vacuum cleaner (clean / dock / stuck modes) |
| **Behavior Tree (BT)** | Hierarchical nodes (Sequence, Selector, Action, Condition) | Modular, scalable, fault-tolerant | Steeper learning curve | Boston Dynamics Spot — manipulation + locomotion tasks |
| **POMDP** | Belief-space planning under partial observability | Handles uncertainty rigorously | Computationally expensive (PSPACE-hard) | Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) in murky water |

**Step 1 — FSM explanation:** An FSM consists of states $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots\}$ and transitions $\delta : S \times E \to S$, triggered by events $E$. Best for **deterministic, safety-critical** systems with few states.

**Step 2 — Behavior Tree explanation:** BTs evaluate conditions and execute actions in a tree structure. Selector nodes (OR) try children in order; Sequence nodes (AND) require all to succeed. Best for **complex, modular, hierarchical** tasks.

**Step 3 — POMDP explanation:** A POMDP models state uncertainty via a *belief distribution* $b(s) = p(s \mid \text{history})$. The optimal policy $\pi^{*}(b)$ maximizes expected discounted reward. Best when **sensor noise is dominant** and full observability cannot be assumed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing $h(n)$ admissibility with consistency.** Many students write "heuristic is small" but forget to state $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ formally — *1 mark lost* per occurrence.
> 2. **Skipping units in $k_{att}$ and $k_{rep}$.** Always write $[k_{att}] = \text{N/m}$ and $[k_{rep}] = \text{N·m}^2$ — KTU examiners *specifically* test unit awareness.
> 3. **Kalman Filter notation mix-up.** Use $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}$ (a priori) vs $\hat{x}_{k\mid k}$ (a posteriori) explicitly. Writing "x hat" alone costs ½ mark.
> 4. **Drawing APF diagrams without arrows.** A potential field solution *without* the force vector diagram $\vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{F}_{total}$ is considered incomplete — lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing SLAM with localization.** SLAM = Simultaneous Localization **and** Mapping. Localization alone assumes a known map. Mixing these up is the most common 14-mark question blunder.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- **Autonomy has 3 pillars:** localization, planning, reactive control. Memorize this triangle.
- **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA)** is the classical 1984 architecture. Modern robots use *hybrid* SPA running in parallel.
- **A\*** = $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Manhattan distance is **admissible** on 4-connected grids; Euclidean is admissible on 8-connected grids.
- **Dijkstra** is A\* with $h(n) = 0$ — slower but always optimal.
- **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** is preferred for **high-dimensional / continuous** configuration spaces (e.g., robot arms).
- **Kalman Filter** = optimal for **linear Gaussian** systems. **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** handles mild non-linearities. **Particle Filter** handles arbitrary, multi-modal distributions.
- **Potential Field** is **complete in static, known environments** but suffers from **local minima** — the biggest known drawback.
- **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** $< 1.5$ s triggers the **reactive override** of the global planner.
- **LIDAR ranging formula** $d = c\,\Delta t / 2$ — the factor 2 is for the **round-trip** travel of the laser pulse. Often forgotten → loses 1 mark.
- **SLAM** is the simultaneous estimation of $\text{bel}(x_t, m)$ — never the same as plain localization.
- **Behavior Trees** are *reactive* — they re-evaluate conditions every tick, unlike FSMs which only transition on events.
- **POMDP** policies operate on **belief states**, not physical states — this is the *single most* tested concept distinction in advanced KTU electives.
